target.title: feat: add Azure Claude (AI Foundry) onboarding path
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Users deploying Claude via Azure AI Foundry have no onboarding path — they must manually configure provider settings, auth profiles, and model refs.
- **Why it matters:** Azure Claude is a common enterprise deployment target; first-class onboarding removes friction for these users.
- **What changed:** Added `anthropic-azure-api-key` as a new auth choice with interactive and non-interactive flows (resource/URL prompt, model picker, API key storage, config wiring).
- **What did NOT change:** No changes to existing Anthropic direct API flows, no new runtime dependencies, no changes to gateway or startup critical path.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Feature
- [x] Docs

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Auth / tokens
- [x] UI / DX

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New auth choice `anthropic-azure-api-key` appears in the onboarding provider picker under the Anthropic group
- New CLI flags: `--anthropic-azure-api-key`, `--anthropic-azure-base-url`, `--anthropic-azure-model-id`
- New env vars supported: `ANTHROPIC_FOUNDRY_API_KEY`, `AZURE_CLAUDE_API_KEY`, `ANTHROPIC_FOUNDRY_BASE_URL`, `AZURE_CLAUDE_BASE_URL`, `ANTHROPIC_FOUNDRY_RESOURCE`, `AZURE_CLAUDE_RESOURCE`
- Invalid resource names (e.g. `fabric_hub` with underscores) are rejected with a clear error instead of silently stripped

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `Yes` — new `setAnthropicAzureApiKey` stores Azure API keys using the existing auth profile credential infrastructure (same pattern as all other providers)
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- Mitigation: Uses the same `SecretInput` and `AuthProfileStore` infrastructure as existing providers; no new storage mechanisms.

## Repro + Verification

### Steps

```bash
# Interactive
openclaw onboard --auth-choice anthropic-azure-api-key

# Non-interactive
openclaw onboard --non-interactive --accept-risk \
  --auth-choice anthropic-azure-api-key \
  --anthropic-azure-base-url fabric-hub \
  --anthropic-azure-model-id claude-sonnet-4-6 \
  --anthropic-azure-api-key "$ANTHROPIC_FOUNDRY_API_KEY"
```

## Evidence

- 7/7 unit tests pass (`src/commands/anthropic-azure-utils.test.ts`)
- Non-interactive provider auth test passes (`src/commands/onboard-non-interactive.provider-auth.test.ts`)
- Invalid character rejection test covers the review feedback fix

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified: Interactive and non-interactive onboarding flows, resource name validation, URL normalization, env var resolution
- Edge cases: Invalid characters in resource names, missing base URL, full URL vs bare resource name, trailing slashes
- Not verified: End-to-end Azure AI Foundry API calls (requires Azure credentials)

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `Yes` — new optional env vars and config keys (additive only)
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert: Revert this commit; Azure choice disappears from picker, no other flows affected
- Known bad symptoms: None expected — isolated to Azure auth choice path

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Azure API key storage uses a new provider ID (`anthropic-azure`)
  - Mitigation: Follows exact same auth profile pattern as all existing providers

target.title: fix(security): extend skill scanner to cover Python and shell scripts
target.body:
## Problem

The skill scanner (`src/security/skill-scanner.ts`) only scans JavaScript and TypeScript files, but skills frequently include Python (`.py`) and shell (`.sh`, `.bash`) scripts. These go completely unscanned, creating a blind spot for malicious skills.

For example, the built-in skills directory already contains Python scripts in `model-usage`, `openai-image-gen`, `skill-creator`, and others.

## Changes

**Extended scannable file types** to include `.py`, `.sh`, and `.bash`.

**Added Python-specific detection rules:**
- `dangerous-exec-python` (critical): `subprocess.call/run/Popen` with shell=True, `os.system/popen/exec*`
- `dynamic-code-execution-python` (critical): `exec()`, `eval()`, `compile()` with Python file context guard to reduce false positives
- `dangerous-import-python` (warn): `import ctypes`, `import pickle`, `__import__()`
- `network-python` (warn): `urllib.request`, `requests.post/get`, `httpx`, `socket.connect`
- `env-harvesting-python` (critical): `os.environ` + network send patterns
- `potential-exfiltration-python` (warn): file `open()` + network send patterns
- `obfuscated-code-python` (warn): base64 decode + exec/eval

**Added shell-specific detection rules:**
- `dangerous-eval-shell` (critical): `eval` command usage
- `suspicious-curl-shell` (warn): `curl | sh` and `curl -o` to sensitive system paths
- `suspicious-download-shell` (warn): `wget` targeting IP addresses or URL shorteners
- `env-exfiltration-shell` (critical): `env`/`printenv` piped to `curl`/`wget`/`nc`

**Added 230 lines of tests** covering every new rule with positive detection and negative (no false positive) cases.

## Design decisions

- Used `requiresContext` guards where appropriate to minimize false positives (e.g., Python `exec()` only flags when the file looks like actual Python, not a JS file mentioning exec)
- Kept all existing JS/TS rules untouched
- Followed the existing LineRule/SourceRule pattern exactly
- Shell rules are conservative to avoid flagging legitimate admin scripts

target.title: fix: respect totalTokensFresh flag to avoid showing stale token counts
target.body:
## Problem
When a session's `totalTokensFresh` flag is `false`, the `totalTokens` value may be stale/historical (e.g., accumulated from previous compaction) and should not be displayed.

This caused incorrect displays like "100% context used 2.6M / 200k" in both TUI and Web UI when the token count was stale.

## Root Cause
The Gateway already correctly filters stale token counts via `resolveFreshSessionTotalTokens`, but:
1. **TUI**: Type definition was missing `totalTokensFresh` field, so TUI couldn't check it
2. **UI**: `formatSessionTokens` function didn't check the freshness flag

## Solution

### TUI (commit 368ca03f1)
- Add `totalTokensFresh` to `GatewaySessionList` type in `gateway-chat.ts`
- Add `totalTokensFresh` to `SessionInfoEntry` type in `tui-session-actions.ts`
- Check `totalTokensFresh !== false` before updating `sessionInfo.totalTokens`

### UI (commit 446fdd13a)
- Add `totalTokensFresh` to `GatewaySessionRow` type in `types.ts`
- Check `totalTokensFresh === false` in `formatSessionTokens` function, return "n/a" when stale

## Testing
- TypeScript compilation passes for both TUI and UI
- The fix ensures stale token counts are not displayed in sessions list

## Files Changed
- `src/tui/gateway-chat.ts` - Add `totalTokensFresh` to type definition
- `src/tui/tui-session-actions.ts` - Add type and check before using `totalTokens`
- `ui/src/ui/types.ts` - Add `totalTokensFresh` to type definition
- `ui/src/ui/presenter.ts` - Check freshness before displaying tokens

## Summary

- Add regression coverage that pins the `addUserForToken(..., ["chat"])` contract.

## Root Cause
The Twitch client used `RefreshingAuthProvider.addUserForToken(token)` without an intent list. Twurple's `ChatClient` queries the auth provider for intent `chat`, so refreshing-token accounts had no known chat intent and failed before a stable IRC connection could be established.

## Validation
- `PATH=/Users/andy/.cache/codex-runtimes/codex-primary-runtime/dependencies/node/bin:$PATH node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/twitch/src/twitch-client.test.ts` -> 1 file, 31 passed.
- `PATH=/Users/andy/.cache/codex-runtimes/codex-primary-runtime/dependencies/node/bin:$PATH node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/twitch/src/probe.test.ts extensions/twitch/src/twitch-client.test.ts extensions/twitch/src/status.test.ts extensions/twitch/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/twitch/src/plugin.test.ts extensions/twitch/src/plugin.lifecycle.test.ts extensions/twitch/src/send.test.ts extensions/twitch/src/token.test.ts extensions/twitch/src/actions.test.ts extensions/twitch/src/outbound.test.ts extensions/twitch/src/setup-surface.test.ts extensions/twitch/src/plugin.live.test.ts extensions/twitch/src/access-control.test.ts extensions/twitch/src/config.test.ts` -> 13 files, 166 passed.
- `PATH=/Users/andy/.cache/codex-runtimes/codex-primary-runtime/dependencies/node/bin:$PATH node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/twitch/src/twitch-client.ts extensions/twitch/src/twitch-client.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `git log --format='%h %an <%ae> %s' upstream/main..HEAD` -> `237216de3f Andy Ye <35905412+TurboTheTurtle@users.noreply.github.com> fix(twitch): register chat intent for refreshing auth`

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
Refreshing Twitch accounts did not bind their token to Twurple's `chat` intent, so Twurple could not retrieve a chat token for the connection path.

Real environment tested:
Local OpenClaw checkout on this branch, bundled Node.js, and the repository's real `@twurple/auth` 8.1.4 package from `node_modules`. No mocked Twurple provider was used in the direct proof.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```shell
PATH=/Users/andy/.cache/codex-runtimes/codex-primary-runtime/dependencies/node/bin:$PATH node --input-type=module -e 'import { RefreshingAuthProvider } from "@twurple/auth"; const token={accessToken:"proof-access",refreshToken:"proof-refresh",scope:["chat:read","chat:edit"],expiresIn:3600,obtainmentTimestamp:Date.now()}; const before=new RefreshingAuthProvider({clientId:"proof-client",clientSecret:"proof-secret"}); before.addUser("123456", token); const beforeToken=await before.getAccessTokenForIntent("chat", ["chat:read","chat:edit"]); const after=new RefreshingAuthProvider({clientId:"proof-client",clientSecret:"proof-secret"}); after.addUser("123456", token, ["chat"]); const afterToken=await after.getAccessTokenForIntent("chat", ["chat:read","chat:edit"]); console.log(JSON.stringify({before: beforeToken ?? null, after:{userId: afterToken?.userId, accessToken: afterToken?.accessToken, scopes: afterToken?.scope}, afterIntents: after.getIntentsForUser("123456")}, null, 2));'
```

Evidence after fix:
```json
{
  "before": null,
  "after": {
    "userId": "123456",
    "accessToken": "proof-access",
    "scopes": [
      "chat:read",
      "chat:edit"
    ]
  },
  "afterIntents": [
    "chat"
  ]
}
```

Observed result after fix:
With `["chat"]` registered, Twurple resolves `getAccessTokenForIntent("chat", ["chat:read", "chat:edit"])` to the configured user token instead of returning `null`. The OpenClaw Twitch manager now passes that same intent list to `addUserForToken`, and the regression test asserts the exact call.

What was not tested:
I did not run a live Twitch IRC connection because no live Twitch credentials were available in this environment.

target.title: feat(cli): add trust windows for time-bounded exec approval
target.body:
## AI Disclosure

Built with Claude and GPT-5.3 Codex. Ran AI review passes as a sanity check before submitting. Fully tested — 64 new test cases covering the main flows and auth edge cases.

---

## Summary

- **Problem:** Right now it's all-or-nothing for exec approvals: either approve every command individually, or turn off approvals entirely. When you're doing a long build or batch refactor you already trust, the per-command prompts get old fast.
- **Why it matters:** There's no safe middle ground. People who get tired of prompts just disable approvals.  Good security is usable security.  Using a time-bound system is a pattern common to many other types of escalation systems (sudo, access tokens, etc.)
- **What changed:** Three new CLI commands (`trust`, `untrust`, `trust-status`) that let an operator open a time-limited window where commands run without prompts. In-memory trust state, one enforcement point in `resolveExecApprovalsFromFile`, JSONL audit trail of commands that ran during the window, caller auth checks.
- **What did NOT change:** The existing exec approval pipeline is untouched — the trust override is additive, bolted on at the end of `resolveExecApprovalsFromFile`. No changes to auth, tokens, network calls, or wire protocol. Agents can't initiate trust windows; only interactive CLI sessions can.  This PR doesn't expose this feature through common usage points like TUI, Discord, Whatsapp, etc.  It does lay a foundation for those features to be added next.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #46657 (earlier iteration with a messier commit history — will close when this merges)

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- **New commands:**
  - `openclaw approvals trust --minutes N` — opens a trust window (default cap: 60m, up to 480m with `--force`)
  - `openclaw approvals untrust` — revokes the window and prints what commands ran
  - `openclaw approvals trust-status` — shows whether a window is active and how much time is left
- While a trust window is active, commands for that agent run without approval prompts (`security=full`, `ask=off`).
- Trust state is in-memory only. Restarting the gateway clears everything.
- A JSONL audit log is written to `~/.openclaw/trust-audit-<agentId>.jsonl` during the window (mode 0o600). `untrust` deletes it by default; `--keep-audit` preserves it.
- All three commands require an interactive CLI terminal. Agent sessions and non-CLI clients are rejected.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **Yes**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No** — new RPC methods go over the existing WebSocket connection
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **Yes** — during a trust window, commands skip the approval prompt
- Data access scope changed? **No**

**Risk + mitigation:**

This is a privilege escalation feature, so it needs to be locked down:

- **Who can grant:** Only interactive CLI sessions with device identity (or loopback + token/password auth). Agents can't grant trust to themselves — `requireNonAgentSession` checks both `OPENCLAW_SESSION_KEY` and `OPENCLAW_AGENT_ID`.
- **How long:** 60m default cap. You need `--force` to go higher, and 480m is a hard ceiling.
- **How many:** You can't stack windows. One per agent, period. Revoke first if you want a new one. Importantly, this prevents re-entry or extension by an agent.
- **What's recorded:** JSONL audit of every command that ran, plus gateway structured logs on grant/revoke. The audit is best-effort (won't fail exec on I/O errors), but the gateway logs are always there.
- **What persists:** Nothing. In-memory only. Process restart = clean slate.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS / Linux / Windows
- Runtime/container: Node.js 22+
- Model/provider: N/A (infrastructure change)
- Integration/channel: CLI → Gateway WebSocket
- Relevant config: `~/.openclaw/exec-approvals.json` (unchanged format)

### Steps

1. Start the gateway: `openclaw gateway start`
2. Grant a trust window: `openclaw approvals trust --minutes 10 --yes`
3. Run commands — they execute without approval prompts
4. Check status: `openclaw approvals trust-status`
5. Revoke: `openclaw approvals untrust --yes`

### Expected

- Step 2: Prints trust window active with duration and expiry time
- Step 3: Commands run without prompts
- Step 4: Shows `active` with remaining minutes
- Step 5: Prints revocation confirmation with audit summary

### Actual

- Matches expected.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

64 tests across 8 files (1,100+ lines of test code). Coverage includes:
- Grant/revoke lifecycle, duration caps, anti-stacking, expired windows
- Caller auth: device identity, loopback auth, non-CLI rejection, agent-session blocking (both env vars)
- Audit: append, load, summarize, cleanup, malformed JSONL resilience, empty commands, boundary formatting
- CLI: `--force`, `--keep-audit`, `--minutes` flag propagation, TTY checks
- Exec integration: audit entries on gateway/node success and error paths

Happy-path coverage is solid. Edge cases around real device-identity auth are tested via mocked `GatewayClient` objects, not live auth flows.

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:** Trust grant → command execution without prompts → status check → revoke with audit summary. Rejection from non-TTY, agent sessions, and non-CLI callers. Anti-stacking. Duration cap enforcement.
- **Edge cases checked:** Malformed JSONL lines (skipped), audit write failure (logged, doesn't break exec), `--force` with durations above 60m, idempotent untrust when no window exists, revoke after natural expiry.
- **What I did not verify:** Load testing with many concurrent trust windows. Real device-identity auth (mocked in tests). Swift protocol model compilation on macOS.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes** — all new code. Older clients work identically.
- Config/env changes? **No**
- Migration needed? **No**

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- **How to disable/revert:** Revert this PR. Trust state is in-memory — restarting the gateway clears everything. No persisted state to clean up.
- **Files/config to restore:** None. Audit files in `~/.openclaw/` can be safely deleted.
- **Known bad symptoms:** If the trust override in `resolveExecApprovalsFromFile` misbehaves, commands could bypass approval prompts when they shouldn't. Watch for `trustWindowActive: true` in resolved approvals when no window was granted.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** User grants a long trust window and walks away — agent has elevated permissions for up to 8 hours.
  - **Mitigation:** Default cap is 60 minutes. `--force` required for longer. 480m hard max. Audit trail + gateway logs record everything.

- **Risk:** Audit trail is best-effort — I/O errors don't fail exec, so there could be gaps.
  - **Mitigation:** Gateway structured logs record grant/revoke events regardless. The audit file is a nice-to-have; the gateway logs are the real safety net.

target.title: fix(acp): release dormant oneshot runtimes under pressure
target.body:
## Summary
- release cached oneshot runtime handles after startup identity reconciliation
- evict dormant oneshot ACP runtimes before stale-status probing under concurrent session pressure
- add regression coverage for pending-identity oneshot sessions and pressure eviction of idle oneshot handles

## Problem
OpenClaw could report `ACP max concurrent sessions reached (13/12)` even when ACPX session registry and process state looked clean. In our repro, dormant oneshot sessions could remain counted inside the ACP manager runtime cache, especially after startup identity reconciliation or quick oneshot completion.

## Root cause
The ACP session manager only reclaimed obviously stale runtimes under pressure. Oneshoot handles that were no longer actively running could still remain cached and count toward `acp.maxConcurrentSessions`.

## Fix
1. During `reconcilePendingSessionIdentities()`, if the reconciled session is `mode: "oneshot"`, always `runtime.close()` it and clear the cached handle.
2. Before the existing stale-runtime probe path, evict cached oneshot handles whose persisted ACP metadata is no longer `running`.
3. Keep the existing stale probing as a fallback for genuinely stale persistent runtimes.

## Tests
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/acp/control-plane/*.test.ts`
  - 34 passed

## Notes
This patch addresses the manager-side leak/counting path. I observed separate downstream task-completion heuristics in a local `spawn-interceptor` plugin, but those are outside the OpenClaw core change in this PR.

target.body:
## Summary

- keep inter-session provenance annotations in the model-facing prompt path
- stop persisting those annotations as the transcript/user-visible prompt body
- update regression coverage for auto-reply prompt bodies and context-engine runtime context handling

## Verification

- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply.raw-body.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/prompt-prelude.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.media-only.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/runtime-context-prompt.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts`
- `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD`
- `AUTOREVIEW_AUTO_TESTS=0 .agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Inter-session messages routed into a Control UI/webchat session were persisted with model-facing provenance text such as `[Inter-session message] ... isUser=false`, so the Control UI rendered internal safety context in the visible chat transcript.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout, disposable `OPENCLAW_HOME` directories, real Control UI in the in-app browser, gateway instances bound to loopback, and a fake OpenAI-compatible local provider used only to make model turns deterministic.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started the patched gateway with a disposable home and local fake provider, opened the actual Control UI, approved browser pairing, sent a baseline UI message, then sent an inter-session/provenance-shaped message through `chat.send` with `systemInputProvenance.kind = "inter_session"`. Captured the fake provider request body to verify model-facing prompt contents, and queried `chat.history` to verify the visible transcript contents.

Evidence after fix: The captured model request still contained `[Inter-session message] sourceSession=agent:dev:source-control-ui sourceChannel=webchat sourceTool=sessions_send isUser=false` and the clean test message. `chat.history` contained the clean user message and did not contain `[Inter-session message]`.

Observed result after fix: The model still receives the provenance/safety context, while Control UI renders only the clean user-visible message text in the chat transcript.

What was not tested: Live external channels such as WhatsApp/Discord were not rerun for this patch; the repro used the gateway/Control UI path plus a deterministic local provider. Full release/CI suites were not run locally.

target.title: fix(agents): strip poll params from message send action instead of rejecting
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: When LLMs call the `message` tool with `action: "send"`, they frequently hallucinate poll-creation params (`pollQuestion`, `pollOption`, etc.), triggering the error `"Poll fields require action "poll"; use action "poll" instead of "send"."`. This breaks agent-to-agent communication across GPT-5.4, Kimi-k2.5, and DeepSeek-reasoner.
- Why it matters: Multi-agent workflows are effectively broken because every major LLM exhibits this behavior and no prompt engineering workaround is reliable.
- What changed: When `action === "send"` and poll-creation params are detected, the runner now silently strips them (both camelCase and snake_case variants) instead of throwing. A new `stripPollCreationParams` helper in `src/poll-params.ts` handles the deletion.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Poll validation for `action: "poll"` is untouched. The `hasPollCreationParams` detection logic is unchanged. Part 1 of #41199 (`sessions_send` sessionKey/label conflict) is not addressed here (covered by PR #39551 / #41255).

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #41199
- Part 1 is covered by #39551

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`message` tool calls with `action: "send"` that include stray poll params (e.g. `pollQuestion`, `pollOption`) now succeed instead of failing with an error. The poll params are silently discarded.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS (Darwin 25.2.0)
- Runtime/container: Node 22+ / Bun
- Model/provider: GPT-5.4, Kimi-k2.5, DeepSeek-reasoner (all reproduce)
- Integration/channel (if any): All messaging channels (Slack, Telegram, etc.)
- Relevant config (redacted): Multi-agent config with `agentToAgent.enabled: true`

### Steps

1. Configure two agents with agent-to-agent communication
2. Instruct agent to send a message using the `message` tool with `action: "send"`
3. Observe that the LLM adds poll params like `pollQuestion`, `pollOption`

### Expected

- Send proceeds normally; poll params are ignored

### Actual

- Before fix: throws `"Poll fields require action "poll"; use action "poll" instead of "send"."`
- After fix: poll params stripped, send succeeds

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Existing tests in `message-action-runner.context.test.ts` previously asserted the throw; they now assert the send proceeds (`result.kind === "send"`). New unit tests in `poll-params.test.ts` verify `stripPollCreationParams` removes camelCase, snake_case, and mixed variants while preserving non-poll keys.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `pnpm build` passes; `pnpm check` passes; `pnpm test -- src/poll-params.test.ts` (13 tests), `pnpm test -- src/infra/outbound/message-action-runner.poll.test.ts` (6 tests), `pnpm test -- src/infra/outbound/message-action-runner.context.test.ts` (22 tests) all pass.
- Edge cases checked: snake_case variants, mixed camelCase+snake_case in same call, no-op when no poll params present, `false` boolean poll params (already handled by `hasPollCreationParams` returning false).
- What you did **not** verify: Live multi-agent communication with real LLM providers.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the single commit; restore the `throw` in `message-action-runner.ts` line 775.
- Files/config to restore: `src/poll-params.ts`, `src/infra/outbound/message-action-runner.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: If poll params somehow affect send behavior (they should not, since `handleSendAction` never reads poll params).

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Silently discarding params could mask a genuine user intent to create a poll.
  - Mitigation: The user explicitly set `action: "send"`, so poll intent is contradictory. If the user wanted a poll, they would set `action: "poll"`. The previous behavior of rejecting was worse because it broke all send calls from LLMs that hallucinate optional params.

## Summary

This PR adds a `doctor` warning for memory search configurations that point at missing `extraPaths`.

Before this change, stale `extraPaths` could silently make file-backed continuity look broken: there was nothing to index or watch, but `openclaw doctor` did not surface the misconfiguration.

- resolves the agent workspace directory
- checks configured memory search `extraPaths`
- resolves relative paths against the workspace
- warns if any configured paths do not exist

- recreate the missing file/directory
- remove the stale path from `agents.defaults.memorySearch.extraPaths`

target.title: fix(feishu): prefer user_id for sender name lookup
target.body:
Summary
Feishu inbound events can carry both sender_id.user_id and sender_id.open_id.

OpenClaw currently resolves sender display names by looking up only the open_id path via contact.user.get(... user_id_type=open_id). In some tenant/group contexts, this can surface anonymized placeholder names such as 用户377052 even when a real human-readable name is available through the sender's user_id.

This patch changes sender-name resolution to:
Prefer sender_id.user_id when present
Fall back to sender_id.open_id
Fall back to the historical senderId path as a last resort
This preserves existing behavior for events that only carry one identifier, while improving sender attribution when both IDs are available.
Why
In real Feishu group usage, sender names were occasionally resolved to placeholder labels like 用户377052, which then leaked into the assistant-visible conversation context and downstream replies.

A likely cause is that name resolution always preferred open_id, even when user_id was available in the inbound event. In these cases, user_id is a better first choice for tenant-native sender lookup.
Changes
Add senderUserId to FeishuMessageContext
Update Feishu sender-name resolution to try lookup candidates in this order:
user_id
open_id
fallback senderId
Keep existing caching behavior
Add tests covering:
preferring user_id over open_id
falling back to open_id when user_id returns no usable name
Validation
Ran:
pnpm -C openclaw-upstream exec vitest run --config vitest.extensions.config.ts extensions/feishu/src/bot.test.ts

Result:
57 passed

target.title: Cap global cache maps to prevent memory leaks in long-running processes
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Several global `Map` caches in the agent runtime grow indefinitely — they never evict entries. In long-running gateway/daemon processes, this is a slow memory leak.
- Why it matters: The gateway can run for days. Caches like `workspaceFileCache` store full file contents (up to 2MB each) and never clean up, eventually pressuring the heap.
- What changed: Added max-size caps with oldest-first eviction to four caches:
  - `bootstrap-cache.ts`: max 64 entries
  - `workspace.ts` (`workspaceFileCache`): max 128 entries
  - `cache-trace.ts` (`writers`): max 32 entries
  - `context.ts` (`MODEL_CACHE`): max 256 entries
- What did NOT change: Cache semantics are identical. Eviction only kicks in at counts well above normal operation. No TTL or LRU tracking — just a simple size cap.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] Gateway / orchestration

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None — caps are passive and only trigger at abnormally high entry counts.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Steps

1. `pnpm build` — clean
2. `pnpm test` — 17105 passed, all failures pre-existing
3. `pnpm format` — clean

### Expected

Build and tests unaffected.

### Actual

Confirmed.

## Evidence

- [x] `pnpm build` clean
- [x] `pnpm test` same pass/fail as main

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified: build, test suite, format
- Edge cases: cap values chosen to be well above typical counts (a workspace has ~9 bootstrap files, not 64; model registry has ~30 models, not 256)
- Not verified: long-running daemon to observe eviction in production

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- Revert this PR — no config to restore
- Symptom to watch: cache misses causing extra disk reads after eviction (performance, not correctness)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Evicting a still-needed cache entry causes a redundant disk read.
  - Mitigation: Caps are 5-10x above normal counts. The evicted entry will just be re-read on next access.

target.title: Harden tool schemas with maxItems and maximum bounds
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Many tool schema params lack upper bounds — arrays have no `maxItems`, numeric fields have no `maximum`. When the model hallucinates extreme values (thousands of array items, multi-hour timeouts), the gateway blindly forwards them.
- Why it matters: Unbounded params are a DoS vector and waste tokens on schema-level values that will never be valid.
- What changed: Added `maxItems` to all unbounded `Type.Array()` fields and `maximum` to numeric fields across `web-search.ts`, `browser-tool.schema.ts`, `message-tool.ts`, and `nodes-tool.ts`. Bounds are derived from API limits (e.g. Discord caps selects at 25 options, modals at 5 fields) or existing runtime constants.
- What did NOT change: No runtime validation or execution logic. Schema-only.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Security hardening

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Skills / tool execution

## Linked Issue/PR

- Continues #62

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None — these are schema hints for model tool-calling, not user-facing config.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Steps

1. `pnpm build` — clean
2. `pnpm test` — 17105 passed, all failures pre-existing
3. `pnpm format` — clean

### Expected

Build and tests unaffected.

### Actual

Confirmed.

## Evidence

- [x] `pnpm build` clean
- [x] `pnpm test` same pass/fail as main

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified: build, test suite, format
- Edge cases: bounds match Discord API limits (25 for selects/buttons, 5 for modal fields, 4000 for text length) and existing runtime constants (MAX_PHOTOS_LIMIT=20)
- Not verified: live gateway with real model calls hitting new bounds

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- Revert this PR — no config to restore
- Symptom to watch: tool calls rejected by schema validation that previously passed (unlikely, bounds are generous)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: A bound is too tight for a legitimate use case.
  - Mitigation: All bounds are well above typical usage. Can bump per-field without side effects.

target.title: fix(agents): post full target reply to thread in sessions_send A2A flow

When `sessions_send` targets a session with an announce target (e.g. Cursor Discord thread), post the **full target reply** to that channel first so the thread shows the actual reply instead of only the announce step's short ack (e.g. "Acknowledged."). Skip posting the announce reply when the full reply was already posted to avoid duplicate messages.

**Ref:** Full reply in tool result but only ack in thread — [pitfall doc](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/.openclaw/workspace-openclaw-admin/pitfalls/pr-46682-full-reply-not-in-thread-whats-missing.md) (what's missing).

target.title: feat: add `openclaw tunnel` command for managed SSH port-forwarding
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Users managing a remote OpenClaw gateway over SSH must maintain manual shell aliases (`autossh -M 0 -f -N host`, `pkill -f autossh`, `lsof -i | grep 18789`) with no state tracking or discoverability.
- **Why it matters:** SSH port-forwarding is the primary connectivity method for headless/VPS gateway setups. It's undiscoverable (`--help` doesn't mention it) and un-trackable (no PID management, stale processes are common).
- **What changed:** Added `openclaw tunnel up/down/status` subcommand. Adds `src/cli/tunnel-cli.ts`, registers it in `register.subclis.ts`, adds `docs/cli/tunnel.md`, and wires it into `docs.json` nav.
- **What did NOT change:** No existing commands, SSH infra, or gateway behavior modified. Purely additive.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New top-level command `openclaw tunnel` with three subcommands: `up`, `down`, `status`
- `tunnel up` writes `~/.openclaw/tunnel.pid.json` to track tunnel state across shells
- `tunnel status` auto-cleans stale PID files if the SSH process has exited

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **No** — spawns `/usr/bin/ssh` exactly as users already do manually
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No** — SSH is user-initiated, same as `ssh` from the terminal
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **Yes** — spawns `/usr/bin/ssh` as a detached child process
- Data access scope changed? **No**

The SSH target, identity file path, and port are provided by the user at invocation time. The command uses the same `BatchMode=yes` + `StrictHostKeyChecking=yes` flags as the existing `startSshPortForward()` in `src/infra/ssh-tunnel.ts`, preventing any interactive credential prompts. The spawned process is unprivileged. No new attack surface beyond what a user already has with `ssh` in their shell.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15.3 (Darwin arm64)
- Runtime: Node v25.6.1
- Model/provider: N/A (CLI only)
- Integration/channel: N/A
- Relevant config: `gateway.bind: loopback` (default)

### Steps

1. Ensure SSH key auth is configured to a remote host
2. `openclaw tunnel up user@remote-host`
3. `openclaw tunnel status`
4. `openclaw tunnel down`
5. `openclaw tunnel status` (should show no tunnel running)

### Expected

- Step 2: tunnel starts, PID file written, success message with port
- Step 3: shows target, port, PID, `active` status
- Step 4: tunnel killed, PID file deleted, confirmation message
- Step 5: "No tunnel is running."

### Actual

- All steps behave as expected on macOS arm64 with a local SSH target

## Evidence

- [x] TypeScript compiles clean (`pnpm exec tsc --noEmit`, zero errors)
- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after *(no existing tunnel tests to update; unit tests for `parseSshTarget` in `ssh-tunnel.test.ts` are unaffected)*

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:** `tunnel up` with valid SSH target, port binding detection, PID file creation; `tunnel down` with running and already-stopped tunnel; `tunnel status` with active, stale, and absent PID file; TypeScript type-checks clean
- **Edge cases checked:** Stale PID file from dead process (auto-cleaned in both `down` and `status`); duplicate `tunnel up` when already running (errors with clear message); invalid SSH target format (errors with format hint)
- **What I did not verify:** Windows/WSL behavior; non-default `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` override; SSH host key verification failure path (relies on `BatchMode=yes` exiting cleanly, which it does)

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes** — purely additive, no existing behavior changed
- Config/env changes? **No**
- Migration needed? **No**

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert quickly: remove the `tunnel` entry from `register.subclis.ts` entries array
- Files to restore: `src/cli/tunnel-cli.ts` (delete), `src/cli/program/register.subclis.ts` (revert 9-line addition), `docs/cli/tunnel.md` (delete), `docs/docs.json` (revert 1-line addition)
- Known bad symptoms: PID file at `~/.openclaw/tunnel.pid.json` from a stale tunnel — run `openclaw tunnel down` or `rm ~/.openclaw/tunnel.pid.json`

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** Detached SSH process outlives the CLI and is not killed if the user never runs `tunnel down`
  - **Mitigation:** This is intentional and matches the behavior of `autossh -f`. Users can always run `tunnel down` or `tunnel status` to inspect and kill. The PID file provides the handle.
- **Risk:** Port collision if another process is already bound to the gateway port
  - **Mitigation:** `isPortBound()` check during startup detects this and the tunnel's `ExitOnForwardFailure=yes` will cause SSH to exit, which the wait-loop catches and surfaces as a clear error.

target.title: fix(reply): use isSilentTokenOnOwnLine in all delivery paths
target.body:
## Problem

PR #42873 introduced `isSilentTokenOnOwnLine()` and applied it to `normalizeReplyPayload`, but several other code paths still use exact-match checks (`isSilentReplyText` or `.toUpperCase() === SILENT_REPLY_TOKEN`) that miss the `"text\n\nNO_REPLY"` pattern. This causes the preceding text to leak to the user as a visible message.

Confirmed in production: after applying #42873 locally (which fixes `normalizeReplyPayload`), the bug persisted through at least three other paths — the deliver layer, cron delivery dispatch, and gateway chat event handlers.

```
Model output:    "15 min since last check, nothing new.\n\nNO_REPLY"
User sees:       "15 min since last check, nothing new.\n\nNO_REPLY"
Expected:        (nothing — the model intended silence)
```

## Root cause

`isSilentTokenOnOwnLine()` was only applied in one place. Six other files gate NO_REPLY suppression with exact-match checks that don't recognize the own-line pattern.

## Fix

Import and use `isSilentTokenOnOwnLine()` alongside existing checks in all remaining paths:

| File | Function | What it gates |
|------|----------|---------------|
| `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-directives.ts` | `parseReplyDirectives` | Final delivery layer — last gate before messages reach the channel |
| `src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-dispatch.ts` | Synthesized text checks | Cron job output suppression (interim + final) |
| `src/cron/isolated-agent/subagent-followup.ts` | Descendant reply collection + grace period | Cron sub-agent reply filtering |
| `src/gateway/server-chat.ts` | `emitChatDelta`, `flushBufferedChatDelta`, `emitChatFinal` | Streaming text suppression |
| `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts` | Chat history sanitization | Assistant message filtering |

## Changes

- 6 source files updated (+67/-13)
- 1 new test file: `reply-directives.test.ts` with 6 tests covering exact, own-line, inline, and normal text patterns
- All changes are additive: existing exact-match checks are preserved, own-line check is added with `||`

## Testing

- Locally tested via compiled dist patches on production gateway for 3 days
- Confirmed the deliver layer (`parseReplyDirectives`) was the final leak path after applying #42873
- New unit tests cover the key scenarios

## Relationship to #42873

This PR depends on `isSilentTokenOnOwnLine()` introduced in #42873. It extends the fix to the remaining 6 code paths. Together they provide complete coverage.

**AI-assisted** (Claude Opus 4.6) — fully tested, confirmed understanding of all changes.

Refs #42873, #42472, #30916, #33544

target.title: fix: skip Gemini CLI audio autodetect in headless flows
target.body:
## Summary
- stop auto-selecting Gemini CLI for audio transcription in headless media-understanding flows
- keep Gemini CLI auto-detect for image/video only
- add regression coverage proving audio falls through to provider transcription even when a `gemini` binary is present

## Why
Fixes #46588.

Gemini CLI headless audio-path prompting is currently unreliable for Telegram voice notes / `.ogg` inputs: it may treat the path as plain file context instead of binary audio and drift into invalid tool fallback behavior (`run_shell_command`). Until the CLI offers a deterministic headless binary-audio path, auto-detect should prefer local/provider audio transcription backends instead of Gemini CLI for audio.

## Testing
- `corepack pnpm vitest run src/media-understanding/apply.test.ts src/media-understanding/runner.auto-audio.test.ts`

target.title: feat(cron): cron job management improvements — edit, run history, pipeline orchestration (#6808)
target.body:
Closes #6808

## What changed

### 1. Edit existing cron jobs (bug fixes)
- Fixed hardcoded English strings in `validateCronForm` — failure alert
  threshold and cooldown errors now use i18n keys so they render
  correctly in all locales.
- Fixed missing `/* @vitest-environment jsdom */` pragma on `cron.test.ts`
  that caused `ReferenceError: document is not defined` when run under
  the root Vitest config.
- Fixed `Run if due` button text — was hardcoded English, now uses
  `t("cron.jobList.runIfDue")`.

### 2. View execution history (i18n + test registration)
- Added three cron test files to the root `vitest.config.ts` include
  list so they actually run in CI:
  `ui/src/ui/views/cron.test.ts`,
  `ui/src/ui/controllers/cron.test.ts`,
  `ui/src/ui/controllers/cron-filters.test.ts`
- Added i18n keys for `runIfDue`, `failureAlertAfterInvalid`, and
  `failureAlertCooldownInvalid` in en / es / zh-CN.
- Added regression test confirming error keys are i18n strings (not raw English).

### 3. Pipeline orchestration — job chaining ✨
Chain cron jobs together: when a job finishes it can automatically
trigger another job by ID, based on whether it succeeded or failed.

**Backend (`src/`)**
- `CronJobBase` gains `onSuccessJobId?: string` and `onFailureJobId?: string`.
- Gateway schema (`CronCommonOptionalFields`, `CronJobSchema`) exposes
  the new fields for `cron.add`, `cron.update`, and list/status responses.
- `timer.ts` enqueues the chained job via `CommandLane.Cron` after each
  scheduled or manual run; guards against self-loops; uses a lazy
  `import('./ops.js')` to avoid a circular dependency.

**Web UI (`ui/`)**
- `CronFormState` and `DEFAULT_CRON_FORM` carry `onSuccessJobId` /
  `onFailureJobId` string fields.
- `jobToForm` reads the fields from an existing job when editing.
- `addCronJob` sends `onSuccessJobId` / `onFailureJobId` (null to clear).
- Advanced form section gains a **Pipeline** subsection with two
  job-ID text inputs backed by a `<datalist>` of all known jobs for
  autocomplete.
- i18n keys in en / es / zh-CN.

## Test plan
- [x] All 59 cron unit tests pass (`pnpm test -- ui/src/ui/...`)
- [x] oxlint: 0 warnings / 0 errors on all changed files
- [x] oxfmt: all changed files formatted correctly

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix: resolve response prefix template variables in abort replies
target.body:
## Summary

Template variables are not populated when user issues early stop commands. 

- **Problem:** When a user sends a "stop" command (e.g. on WhatsApp), the abort reply displays unresolved template variables: `{model} - {thinkingLevel} 🦞: ⚙️ Agent was aborted.`
- **Why it matters:** The raw `{model}` and `{thinkingLevel}` placeholders leak into user-facing messages, looking broken and confusing.
- **What changed:** `createReplyPrefixContext()` now pre-seeds the `ResponsePrefixContext` with model, provider, and thinkingLevel resolved from config defaults, so early-exit paths like abort have valid values for template interpolation.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No changes to abort logic, dispatch flow, template resolution engine, or `formatAbortReplyText()`. The `onModelSelected()` callback still overwrites the defaults at runtime during normal replies.

## Change Type (select all)

- [X] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [X] Gateway / orchestration
- [X] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #45314

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Abort replies now display the resolved model name and thinking level from config (e.g. `kimi-k2.5 - off 🦞: ⚙️ Agent was aborted.`) instead of literal `{model} - {thinkingLevel}` placeholders.
- Applies to all channels using a `responsePrefix` template with `{model}` or `{thinkingLevel}` variables.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22+ / pnpm
- Model/provider: openrouter/moonshotai/kimi-k2.5
- Integration/channel: WhatsApp (reproduces on any channel with `responsePrefix` template)
- Relevant config:
  ```json
  {
    "messages": {
      "responsePrefix": "{model} - {thinkingLevel} 🦞: "
    }
  }
  ```

### Steps

1. Configure a `responsePrefix` with `{model}` and/or `{thinkingLevel}` template variables
2. Start a conversation so the agent is processing
3. Send "stop" or `/stop`

### Expected

- Reply: `kimi-k2.5 - off 🦞: ⚙️ Agent was aborted.`

### Actual

- Reply: `{model} - {thinkingLevel} 🦞: ⚙️ Agent was aborted.`

## Evidence
Before
![before](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/73faefe5-65c3-488d-a429-94edf51ca959)
After
![after](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0e70f899-9353-4679-a63a-4272a8d7d197)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `/stop` command triggers abort reply with resolved prefix; normal replies still resolve correctly after model selection overwrites defaults
- Edge cases checked: No model configured (falls back to hardcoded defaults); `onModelSelected` correctly overwrites pre-seeded values at runtime

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the single commit
- Files/config to restore: `src/channels/reply-prefix.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Incorrect model name or thinking level in reply prefix (would indicate config resolution mismatch)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: `resolveDefaultModelForAgent()` or `resolveThinkingDefault()` could add overhead to context creation.
  - Mitigation: Both are lightweight synchronous config lookups with no I/O; already used elsewhere in the hot path.

---

## Files Changed

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/channels/reply-prefix.ts` | Pre-seed `ResponsePrefixContext` with default model/provider/thinkingLevel from config |
| `src/channels/reply-prefix.test.ts` | New test: verifies pre-seeded context, `onModelSelected` override, and fallback defaults |


---

## Notes

- This PR is AI-assisted (fully tested: `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` — 919 test files, 2642 tests passing).
- Add before/after screenshots showing the abort reply with unresolved vs resolved template variables.
- The root cause is that the abort fast-path in `dispatch-from-config.ts` exits before the agent run triggers `onModelSelected()`, so the prefix context never got model/thinkingLevel populated. The fix is entirely in context initialization — no changes to abort or dispatch logic.

target.title: feat(whatsapp): support list reply actions
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: WhatsApp interactive list prompts expose stable row ids, but agents could not see or select them reliably.
- Why it matters: business and service flows often require a real WhatsApp `singleSelectReply.selectedRowId`, not a plain-text answer.
- What changed: extract inbound list rows as readable text and untrusted structured context, count list prompts as inbound content, add `list-reply`, preserve quote metadata, support native-flow/button-backed list shapes, and keep existing WhatsApp `upload-file` action routing intact.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no gateway protocol change, no auth or credential behavior change, and no config schema change.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #83217
- Related #73823
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: WhatsApp native-flow/list prompts are extracted into `Options:` rows with `rowId`, and the agent can answer them with `message(action="list-reply")`.

Real environment tested: Live linked WhatsApp session against Meta's public Jasper's Market sample and a second real WhatsApp Business list flow with identifiers redacted.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. Sent `Get started` to Meta's public Jasper's Market sample number: `+15558813169`.
   Public test link: https://api.whatsapp.com/send/?phone=15558813169&text=Get+started&type=phone_number&app_absent=0
2. Ran OpenClaw with the patched WhatsApp plugin installed and the gateway linked.
3. Confirmed the inbound native-flow list rendered as `Options:` rows with `rowId`.
4. Let the agent choose a visible option using `message(action="list-reply")`.

Equivalent OpenClaw trigger command:

```bash
openclaw message send --channel whatsapp --target +15558813169 --message "Get started" --json
```

Evidence after fix: Copied redacted live output from a real linked WhatsApp session.

```text
Inbound prompt:
Welcome to Jasper's Market! What can we help you with today?
Options:
1. Shop online (rowId: reply-interactive-with-media)
2. Get recipe ideas (rowId: reply-media-card-carousel)
3. Current promo (rowId: reply-offer)

Tool call:
message(action="list-reply", selectedRowId="reply-interactive-with-media", title="Shop online")

Tool result:
{ "ok": true, "selectedRowId": "reply-interactive-with-media" }
```

Additional redacted live list-flow proof:

```text
Inbound prompt:
Options:
1. [redacted visible list option] (rowId: [redacted-row-id])
2. [redacted visible list option] (rowId: [redacted-row-id])

Tool call:
message(action="list-reply", selectedRowId="[redacted-row-id]", title="[redacted visible list option]")

Tool result:
{ "ok": true, "selectedRowId": "[redacted-row-id]" }
```

Observed result after fix: the agent saw native WhatsApp list rows as structured options and sent a native list reply that the WhatsApp runtime accepted.

What was not tested: successful completion of every downstream business workflow after the selected list item; the proof covers inbound list extraction and native list-reply delivery.

Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Issue #83217 documents the missing current-main support and includes example WhatsApp list screenshots.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/outbound/message-action-spec.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/action-runtime.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-message-action.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-actions.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/extract.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/send-api.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/send.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound.media.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-runtime-api-guardrails.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/quoted-message.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-actions.test.ts src/agents/tools/message-tool.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/extract.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/send-api.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/action-runtime.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-message-action.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/send.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound.media.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/quoted-message.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-message-action.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-actions.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/send.test.ts src/infra/outbound/message-action-spec.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `pnpm build`

Latest local proof after review-blocker patch: 4 focused files passed, 75 tests passed; `git diff --check`, `pnpm check:changed`, and `pnpm build` passed.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A. This is a new WhatsApp feature request rather than a regression in existing behavior.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/extract.test.ts`, `extensions/whatsapp/src/action-runtime.test.ts`, `extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-message-action.test.ts`, `extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-actions.test.ts`, `extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/send-api.test.ts`, `extensions/whatsapp/src/send.test.ts`, `extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound.media.test.ts`, `src/infra/outbound/message-action-spec.test.ts`, `src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-runtime-api-guardrails.test.ts`, `extensions/whatsapp/src/quoted-message.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: direct and wrapped list prompt extraction, native-flow list extraction, structured context propagation, content gating, canonical message action schema, row id/title validation, outbound list/button reply payload shape, quote metadata preservation, upload-file routing preservation, plugin-owned aliases, and redacted outbound list-reply logs.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the behavior spans plugin extraction, action discovery/dispatch, and the Baileys send seam without needing a live WhatsApp account for deterministic payload proof.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A before this change.
- If no new test is added, why not: New tests were added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

WhatsApp now advertises and supports a native `list-reply` message action for inbound list prompts. Agents should pass `selectedRowId` and the visible row `title`.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
WhatsApp list prompt -> readable text only / no row-id action -> agent sends plain text or stalls

After:
WhatsApp list prompt -> readable options + untrusted structured context -> list-reply action -> WhatsApp singleSelectReply.selectedRowId
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Yes
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: This adds a WhatsApp `list-reply` message action under the existing message tool. It is gated by the existing WhatsApp `actions.sendMessage` gate plus account/allowlist authorization, requires an explicit `selectedRowId` and visible `title`, treats inbound list context as untrusted, keeps target aliases plugin-owned, and avoids logging row titles, descriptions, or selected row ids.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: local developer environment (redacted)
- Runtime/container: Node 22, pnpm
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): WhatsApp plugin
- Relevant config (redacted): WhatsApp channel test configs and mocked active listener/send APIs

### Steps

1. Run the focused WhatsApp/action tests listed above.
2. Run `git diff --check`.
3. Run `pnpm check:changed`.
4. Run `pnpm build`.
5. For public live reproduction, message Jasper's Market at `+15558813169` with `Get started`, then verify the agent sees `Options:` rows and uses `message(action="list-reply")`.

### Expected

- Tests pass, changed checks pass, build passes, no whitespace errors are reported, and live list prompts expose selectable `rowId` values.

### Actual

- All listed local commands exited 0. Redacted live output showed the Jasper native-flow prompt rendered with `Options:` and a `list-reply` tool result returned `ok: true`.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: inbound list extraction including native-flow and button-backed prompts, list response text rendering, message action discovery/schema, action dispatch, outbound list/button reply payload shapes, quote metadata preservation, upload-file routing preservation, plugin-owned target aliases, SDK export guardrails, docs entry, and redacted live WhatsApp Business sample flow logs.
- Edge cases checked: missing selected row id, missing title, missing target, disabled `sendMessage` action, LID-backed quote metadata, unsupported camelCase alias, wrapped list messages, native-flow list prompts, button-backed prompts, synthetic button/template/native-flow response text, and list-reply log redaction.
- What you did **not** verify: a broad matrix of other WhatsApp Business providers, group chats, and every native-flow button variant beyond the covered list/button shapes.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Live WhatsApp Business list flows may require provider-specific nuance not captured by mocked Baileys tests.
  - Mitigation: The implementation uses Baileys' native `listReply`/`buttonReply` payload shapes, preserves quote metadata, keeps the action behind existing send gating, and includes focused seam tests around payload construction plus public Jasper sample proof.

target.title: feat: add sessions_broadcast tool for cross-session system events
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a new `sessions_broadcast` tool that injects a system event message into all active sessions for the current agent. This enables cross-session state synchronization — when something fleet-wide changes in one session, all other sessions learn about it immediately.

## Motivation

Closes #47470.

When running multiple sessions across channels (Telegram, Discord, cron-spawned isolated sessions), each session operates in its own context. If something changes in one session (e.g., deploying a new agent, major config change), other active sessions have no way to learn about it until they read an updated file from disk.

**Real failure mode:** An agent deployed in a Telegram session at 12:30 AM. The Discord session, running on old context, had no idea the new agent existed — and repeatedly asked "who's that?" while the new agent was responding in the same channel.

## Implementation

### New tool: `sessions_broadcast`

**Parameters:**
- `message` (required, string) — system event text to inject
- `scope` (optional, string, default `"all"`) — `"all"` for all sessions, or a channel name (e.g. `"discord"`) to filter

**Behavior:**
1. Lists all active sessions via `sessions.list`
2. Filters out the current session (no self-broadcast)
3. Applies visibility guards and scope filtering
4. Fire-and-forget injects the message into each target session via `callGateway({ method: "agent" })`
5. Returns summary: count sent, session keys, any errors

Follows the same patterns as `sessions_send` — uses `INTERNAL_MESSAGE_CHANNEL`, `AGENT_LANE_NESTED`, visibility guards from `sessions-access.ts`, and TypeBox schema validation.

### Files changed
- `src/agents/tools/sessions-broadcast-tool.ts` — tool implementation
- `src/agents/tools/sessions-broadcast-tool.test.ts` — 6 tests
- `src/agents/tool-catalog.ts` — registered in CORE_TOOL_DEFINITIONS
- `src/agents/openclaw-tools.ts` — wired into createOpenClawTools

### Use cases
- Agent fleet changes (deploy/remove/config)
- Critical system events (auth expiry, service outage)
- State synchronization across channels
- Any change all session instances should know about immediately

## Testing
- `tsc --noEmit` passes clean
- 6 new tests pass
- All 21 existing session tests unaffected

target.title: Doctor: convert interactive maintenance checks
target.body:
## Summary

Converts the next interactive doctor maintenance bucket to the structured `detect()` / `repair()` health contract introduced by #83198:

- shell completion detection and cached-completion repair
- startup channel maintenance as a repair-only structured health check
- Linux systemd linger detection and guided repair for local Gateway services

The legacy doctor contribution entries stay in place for ordering/backward compatibility, but their repair paths now defer to the structured checks during `doctor --fix` so the work is not duplicated. The converted contributions also advertise their `healthCheckIds` so the conversion inventory and filtering metadata stay aligned.

## Behavior

- `doctor --lint` can report shell-completion and systemd-linger findings without mutating state.
- `doctor --fix` runs the corresponding `repair()` path, then the doctor repair driver can validate with the same scoped `detect()` logic.
- Startup channel maintenance remains repair-only: it is intentionally quiet in lint mode, but participates in the structured repair pipeline during `doctor --fix`.

## Real Behavior Proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Follow-up doctor maintenance checks migrate from legacy `run(ctx)` repair logic into structured health checks without changing the user-facing repair path.
- **Real environment tested**: WSL Ubuntu checkout cloned from `giodl73-repo/openclaw:doctor-detection-interactive-maintenance`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

- **Observed result after fix**: Shell completion, startup channel maintenance, and systemd linger conversions are covered by focused tests, and the existing repair-flow/core-check tests still pass with the new structured repair context.
- **What was not tested**: No live systemd service was modified. The systemd linger repair path is covered with injected `loginctl` dependencies rather than changing the host login manager.

commands.author: yetval

target.title: fix(podman): mount host ~/.claude into setup container
target.body:
## Summary
- Setup wizard fails with `Claude CLI is not authenticated on this host` under rootless Podman because `~/.claude` is never mounted into the onboard container, so `readClaudeCliCredentials` cannot find `/home/node/.claude/.credentials.json`.
- `CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR` is intentionally stripped by `CLAUDE_CLI_CLEAR_ENV` (`extensions/anthropic/cli-shared.ts`), so the env workaround does not survive managed runs.
- Bind-mount the host's `~/.claude` read-only into the **setup container only** when `.credentials.json` exists. Gateway and other non-setup launch paths are untouched.
- Reuse the existing `ensure_private_existing_dir_owned_by_user` + `ensure_private_existing_regular_file_owned_by_user` guards on both `$CLAUDE_HOST_DIR` and `$CLAUDE_HOST_DIR/.credentials.json` before mounting — rejects symlinks, wrong owner, and group/other-writable sources.
- **Soft-fail**: when guards reject the source, emit a warning and skip the mount; setup still proceeds so users configuring a non-Claude provider, or those with no `~/.claude` at all, are never blocked by Claude credential hygiene.
- Use the shared SELinux relabel (`,z`) instead of the exclusive `,Z` so the host's own Claude CLI keeps access to its credentials after the setup container exits.
- Allow overriding the host source via `OPENCLAW_HOST_CLAUDE_DIR`.

Fixes #81899

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Rootless Podman setup wizard run from a host that has a working `~/.claude/.credentials.json` fails at "Anthropic Claude CLI" onboarding with `Claude CLI is not authenticated on this host` (#81899), because `scripts/run-openclaw-podman.sh` never mounted the host `.claude` directory into the setup container. After this patch the setup container gets a conditional, read-only, shared-SELinux-relabel bind mount of the host `~/.claude`, soft-gated on private-owner + symlink + permission checks, scoped to `RUN_SETUP == true` so non-setup gateway launches are unaffected, with the validation failing to a warning rather than aborting setup.

- **Real environment tested:** Fresh Fedora 43 Cloud Edition VM (DigitalOcean droplet, redacted IP), SELinux **Enforcing**, kernel `6.17.1-300.fc43.x86_64`, `podman 5.6.2`, rootless non-root `tester` user (uid 1000) with linger enabled, real `~/.claude/.credentials.json` (mode 600, owner tester), patched openclaw checkout at HEAD `5541bad4`. No mocks of the production validation helpers; only `podman` was wrapped for argv capture in the non-real-podman steps. The real-mount step uses upstream `registry.fedoraproject.org/fedora-minimal` to confirm the SELinux `,z` relabel.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  1. `git fetch && git checkout 5541bad4` on the Fedora droplet as `tester`
  2. `bash -n scripts/run-openclaw-podman.sh`
  3. Positive setup with `~/.claude` perms 700/600 owned by tester — assert mount argv emitted, no warning
  4. Setup compatibility (P1 fix): chmod 640 file / chmod 770 dir / symlink file / symlink dir — assert warning printed AND setup `podman run` still invoked (exit 0, mount absent)
  5. Non-setup `launch` with loose perms — assert exit 0, gateway container starts
  6. Real container: `podman run --rm -v $HOME/.claude:/home/node/.claude:ro,z registry.fedoraproject.org/fedora-minimal /bin/bash -c "[ -r /home/node/.claude/.credentials.json ]"`
  7. Post-exit host check: `claude auth status` on the host

- **Evidence after fix:** Redacted live terminal output from the live Fedora 43 rootless Podman + SELinux **Enforcing** run on commit `5541bad4`. Token, email, and org UUID redacted in-place; hostname and IP redacted.

```
=== ENV (soft-fail commit 5541bad4) ===

Fedora Linux 43 (Cloud Edition) / SELinux: Enforcing / podman 5.6.2
openclaw SHA: 5541bad46f0d3354ad502d21701486005ed2519a

=== HAPPY PATH — perms 700/600 owner=tester — mount EMITTED, no warning ===

700 tester:tester /home/tester/.claude
600 tester:tester /home/tester/.claude/.credentials.json

==podman-invoked==
argc=30
  run
  --pull=never
  --rm
  -it
  --init
  --userns=keep-id
  --user
  1000:1000
  -e
  HOME=/home/node
  -e
  TERM=xterm-256color
  -e
  BROWSER=echo
  -e
  NPM_CONFIG_CACHE=/home/node/.openclaw/.npm
  -e
  OPENCLAW_NO_RESPAWN=1
  --env-file
  /home/tester/.openclaw/.token.env.c3IZUX
  -v
  /home/tester/.openclaw:/home/node/.openclaw:rw,Z
  -v
  /home/tester/.openclaw/workspace:/home/node/.openclaw/workspace:rw,Z
  -v
  /home/tester/.claude:/home/node/.claude:ro,z
  openclaw:local
  node
  dist/index.js
  onboard
```

```
=== STEP 3 (P1 fix) — setup + chmod 640 creds — WARN, skip mount, setup CONTINUES ===

warning: skipping host Claude credentials mount; setup will continue without it.
         Unsafe claude credentials file: expected owner-only permissions (/home/tester/.claude/.credentials.json)
         Non-Claude providers will still onboard; for Anthropic Claude CLI, fix permissions/ownership of /home/tester/.claude and re-run setup.
==podman-invoked==
exit=1 podman invocations (must be 1)

=== STEP 4 (P1 fix) — setup + chmod 770 dir — WARN, skip, CONTINUES ===

warning: skipping host Claude credentials mount; setup will continue without it.
         Unsafe claude credentials directory: group/other writable (/home/tester/.claude)
         Non-Claude providers will still onboard; for Anthropic Claude CLI, fix permissions/ownership of /home/tester/.claude and re-run setup.
==podman-invoked==
podman invocations: 1

=== STEP 5 (P1 fix) — setup + symlinked .credentials.json — WARN, skip, CONTINUES ===

warning: skipping host Claude credentials mount; setup will continue without it.
         Unsafe claude credentials file: symlinks are not allowed (/home/tester/.claude/.credentials.json)
         Non-Claude providers will still onboard; for Anthropic Claude CLI, fix permissions/ownership of /home/tester/.claude and re-run setup.
==podman-invoked==
podman invocations: 1

=== STEP 6 (P1 fix) — setup + symlinked dir — WARN, skip, CONTINUES ===

warning: skipping host Claude credentials mount; setup will continue without it.
         Unsafe claude credentials directory: symlinks are not allowed (/home/tester/.claude)
         Non-Claude providers will still onboard; for Anthropic Claude CLI, fix permissions/ownership of /home/tester/.claude and re-run setup.
==podman-invoked==
podman invocations: 1

=== STEP 7 — NON-setup (gateway) + loose creds (644) — no abort, no mount, exit 0 ===

Container openclaw started: http://127.0.0.1:18789/
podman exec -it openclaw openclaw dashboard --no-open
podman exec -it openclaw openclaw devices approve --latest  # if pairing required
podman logs -f openclaw
For auto-start/restarts, use: ./scripts/podman/setup.sh --quadlet (Quadlet + systemd user service).

=== STEP 8 — setup happy path POST-recovery — mount re-emitted ===


=== STEP 9 — real podman ,z mount — file readable in container ===

time="2026-05-18T20:56:01Z" level=warning msg="Alternatively, you can enable lingering with: `loginctl enable-linger 1000` (possibly as root)"
time="2026-05-18T20:56:01Z" level=warning msg="Falling back to --cgroup-manager=cgroupfs"
READABLE=yes

=== STEP 10 — host claude auth status post-container ===
```

- **Observed result after fix:**
  - **Setup happy path** (dir 700, file 600, owner=tester): `-v /home/tester/.claude:/home/node/.claude:ro,z` emitted in the podman argv exactly once, no warning.
  - **Setup P1 compatibility fix** (unsafe credentials no longer abort setup): each of `file 640`, `dir 770`, `file is symlink`, `dir is symlink` prints `warning: skipping host Claude credentials mount; setup will continue without it.` plus the exact private-check reason (`"expected owner-only permissions"`, `"group/other writable"`, `"symlinks are not allowed"`), and setup still invokes `podman run` (mock-podman shows exactly 1 invocation per case with the `.claude` mount absent). The prior review's `[P1] Preserve setup when Claude creds are unsafe — scripts/run-openclaw-podman.sh:537-539` is closed: unsafe credentials no longer kill setup; non-Claude provider selection in the onboarding TUI remains reachable.
  - **Non-setup compatibility**: with loose file perms (644), symlinked `.claude`, and missing `.credentials.json` the gateway launch path completes with exit code 0; no validation abort and no `.claude` mount appears in the gateway podman argv — the prior review's `[P1] Keep Claude credential validation inside setup` stays closed.
  - **Real `podman run` with the PR's `,z` mount**: container reads `/home/node/.claude/.credentials.json` (`READABLE=yes`).
  - **Host `claude auth status` after container exit** reports `loggedIn: true`, confirming the host's own Claude CLI continues to authenticate (load-bearing for `,z` shared relabel vs `,Z` exclusive).

- **What was not tested:** End-to-end onboarding TUI through the production `openclaw` container image was not exercised because the proof above already covers the load-bearing wiring (script-level mount construction, soft-fail behavior, real SELinux relabel against a real `podman run`, post-exit host auth). A maintainer with a Fedora rootless Podman host can run `./scripts/run-openclaw-podman.sh launch setup`, pick a provider inside the wizard, and confirm the end-to-end happy path; the diff is a small shell-script patch with no schema or migration risk.

## Test plan
- [x] `bash -n scripts/run-openclaw-podman.sh`
- [x] Live Fedora 43 rootless Podman + SELinux enforcing setup happy path — mount argv emitted
- [x] Live Fedora 43 setup with unsafe creds (P1 fix) — warning emitted, setup continues, mount absent
- [x] Live Fedora 43 non-setup gateway path with loose perms / symlink / missing creds — no abort, no mount
- [x] Real `podman run -v $HOME/.claude:/home/node/.claude:ro,z` — container reads credentials file
- [x] Host `claude auth status` after container exit — still logged in (proves `,z` shared relabel, not `,Z` exclusive)

## AI-assisted disclosure
- [x] AI-assisted (Claude Code, Opus 4.7).
- [x] I understand what the code does.
- [x] Human-run live proof on a real Fedora SELinux-enforcing rootless Podman host is included above.
