target.title: feat(android): add Wear OS app MVP
target.body: ## Summary > This is the follow up PR of #46279 > It was heavily written with the help of Gemini 3.1 Pro (mainly for building UI) and Codex GPT 5.4, including lots of review iterations. > During the implementation of the previous PR I moved more and more logic into a shared layer, which caused lots of review comments about code I didn't write. I tried to let Codex write regression tests for several of these cases to ensure that I don't break features. > Manual testing with my Pixel Watch 2 and Pixel 8 Pro was part of the whole process. Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: OpenClaw did not have a Wear OS companion. - Why it matters: The watch needs a compact, reliable chat surface with fast reply actions, honest connection state, and behavior that still works on tiny round screens and when the app is backgrounded. - What changed: Added a new Wear OS app module, phone-side Wear proxy service for Tailscale support, direct watch gateway client, transcript-first chat UI, session picker, split settings screens, tile + complication support, background reply notifications with quick reply. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not change gateway orchestration semantics, tools/skills behavior, desktop/iOS/macOS clients, or the Android phone app's broader UI theme beyond the phone-side Wear proxy integration. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # (none) - Related # (none) ## User-visible / Behavior Changes List user-visible changes (including defaults/config). If none, write `None`. - New Wear OS companion app with a transcript-first chat flow and a two-page swipe layout. - Chat opens on the transcript page and follows the latest message by default; the primary edge action switches between `Reply` and `Latest`. - Added session picker, chat settings, connection settings, reconnect flow, and direct-vs-phone-proxy connection support on the watch. - Added Wear tile and complication entry points. - Added native watch notifications for background replies, including quick reply via text/voice dictation. - Added optional TTS playback and configurable screen-awake behavior on the watch. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes`) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes`) - New/changed network calls? (`Yes`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`) - Data access scope changed? (`Yes`) - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: The Wear app adds `POST_NOTIFICATIONS`, Wear Data Layer messaging, direct gateway WebSocket access, and storage of watch-side gateway config including token-based direct auth. Risk is constrained by using the existing authenticated gateway paths, limiting watch data access to the same session/gateway calls already exposed by the phone client, honoring platform notification permission, and keeping watch auth/config local to the Wear app. Phone-proxy traffic stays within the paired phone/watch channel and only proxies requests through the phone's active operator session. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Android phone + Wear OS 3/4 watches; manually tested on Pixel Watch 2 and Wear round emulators (small + large) - Runtime/container: Android Studio/Gradle debug builds - Model/provider: Existing OpenClaw gateway via phone proxy and direct gateway modes - Integration/channel (if any): Wear Data Layer between paired phone and watch - Relevant config (redacted): Matching debug-signed phone + watch builds; direct mode host/port/token/TLS when used; phone-proxy mode with phone app installed and signed the same way ### Steps 1. Build and install matching debug phone and Wear builds, then launch the Wear app on a paired watch. 2. Verify transcript flow, reply actions, session switching, settings, direct mode, phone-proxy mode, tile/complication entry points, and reopen/background behavior. 3. Send assistant replies while the watch app is foregrounded and backgrounded, then verify reply notifications, quick reply, reconnect/disconnect behavior, and latest-message scrolling. ### Expected - The watch should show a compact, reliable chat UX with correct connection state, preserved transcript state, working reply paths, and background reply notifications. ### Actual - Matches expected in manual testing on Pixel Watch 2 and both small/large Wear emulators. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) Actual screenshots from Pixel Watch 2: | | | | |---|---|---| | Empty tanscript view | Empty transcript view | Swipe over to menu | | Scroll down in menu | Scroll further down | Click behavior settings button | | Scroll down | Click screen awake setting | Click screen awake setting again | | Go back and enter connection settings | Scroll down to helper text | Disable phone proxy | | Scroll down to gateway config | Scroll further down | Go back to transcript view | | Scroll down to see reply edge button (*default view* if you open the app≈) | Reply button toggles voice input (or let's you pick first, if disabled in settings) | Thinking animation | | When closing app, notification is delivered| Tile adding dialog with preview of tile | Actual tile | | Watch face with complication at top and bottom right (different formats) | ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: Manually tested on a Pixel Watch 2 via Tailscale phone proxy and in both small and large round Wear emulators. Verified chat launch, latest-message behavior, reply flow, session picker, chat settings, connection settings, direct gateway mode, phone-proxy mode, tile launch, complication launch, background reply notifications, and quick reply from the notification. - Edge cases checked: Reopening the app after backgrounding, disconnect/reconnect flow, switching away from and back to the transcript page, scrolling away from latest and jumping back, and testing on both small and large round watch layouts. - What you did **not** verify: every possible Android OEM pairing/background-policy variant, and exhaustive real-network failure cases beyond the implemented reconnect/error handling paths. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`Yes`) - Config/env changes? (`No`) - Migration needed? (`No`) - If yes, exact upgrade steps: None. ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the Wear branch commits or ship only the phone build without installing the Wear module; on-device, switch the watch back to phone-proxy or disconnect direct mode if direct gateway behavior is problematic. - Files/config to restore: `apps/android/wear/**`, `apps/android/app/src/main/java/ai/openclaw/app/wear/WearProxyService.kt`, `apps/android/app/src/main/java/ai/openclaw/app/NodeRuntime.kt`, `apps/android/app/src/main/AndroidManifest.xml`, and `apps/android/app/build.gradle.kts`. - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: watch stuck showing connected when the proxy/direct gateway is not actually usable, sent messages disappearing after history refresh, explicit disconnect reconnecting by itself, reply notification not surfacing or lacking quick reply, and transcript reopening at the wrong position. ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. - Risk: Phone-proxy mode depends on both Wear Data Layer reachability and the phone app having a live operator session. - Mitigation: The watch exposes direct mode as an alternative, surfaces connection state in the UI, and includes reconnect/disconnect controls. - Risk: The second page is dense on the smallest round watches, especially with long session names/keys. - Mitigation: Manual testing covered both small and large round emulator sizes and a real Pixel Watch 2. - Risk: Background reply notifications depend on platform permission and Wear notification presentation. - Mitigation: The app requests notification permission explicitly, and manual verification covered notification delivery plus quick reply behavior.

target.title: fix(feishu): add searchable streaming fallback
target.body:
## Summary
- add an opt-in `channels.feishu.streamingSearchFallback` setting
- send the final streamed Feishu card text as a plain text fallback when enabled so Feishu conversation search can index it
- document the Feishu search limitation and visible duplicate-message tradeoff

Fixes #74767

## Tests
- `pnpm test extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/streaming-card.test.ts`
- `pnpm test extensions/feishu/src/tools-config.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/policy.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/accounts.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.ts extensions/feishu/src/streaming-card.ts extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.test.ts docs/channels/feishu.md extensions/feishu/src/config-schema.ts extensions/feishu/openclaw.plugin.json`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Feishu users searching their conversation history for content from streamed assistant replies could not find any matches (#74767), because OpenClaw's Feishu streaming path closes the final text into a CardKit `interactive` message (`msg_type: "interactive"`), and Feishu's conversation-search index does not cover `interactive` card content — only `text` and `post` messages are indexed per https://open.feishu.cn/document/server-docs/im-v1/message/create.

Real environment tested: macOS 25.3.0, real OpenClaw checkout at `/private/tmp/74808-rebase` (clone of `yetval/openclaw:fix/feishu-streaming-search-fallback` rebased onto upstream `openclaw/main`), node 22 + pnpm v11.1.0, fresh `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`, repro and payload capture run via `pnpm exec tsx` against the real production `buildFeishuPostMessagePayload` helper at `extensions/feishu/src/send.ts` — no mocks, no stubs, no live Feishu credentials exercised.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```
pnpm exec tsx repro-74767.ts
```
where `repro-74767.ts` imports the production `buildFeishuPostMessagePayload` (the helper `sendMessageFeishu` uses to construct outbound payloads), constructs a representative streamed assistant turn, and captures both the BEFORE outbound payload (current main's card-only `streaming.close` path, msg_type `interactive`) and the AFTER outbound payload (this PR's `sendSearchableStreamingFallback` path, which routes the same text through `sendMessageFeishu` and therefore through the search-indexable post path). A deterministic check against Feishu's documented searchable msg_type set (`text`, `post`) then prints whether each captured payload would be indexed.

Evidence after fix: copied live terminal output (full payload-capture run, no redaction needed) from the rebased branch:

```
$ pnpm exec tsx repro-74767.ts
=== BEFORE (current main; card-only, not search-indexable) ===
{
  "msgType": "interactive",
  "content": "{\"schema\":\"2.0\",\"type\":\"card_id\",\"data\":{\"card_id\":\"ckcrd_streaming_demo\",\"template_variable\":{\"text\":\"Here is what I found in the deployment runbook:\\n\\n- ssh into the bastion via `ssh deploy@bastion.prod.example.com`\\n- run `./scripts/rollout.sh canary --region=us-east-1`\\n- watch `./scripts/health-check.sh --watch` until p99 latency returns to baseline\\n\\nCite this in #incident-2026-05-15.\"}}}"
}

=== AFTER (this PR; opt-in fallback adds a search-indexable text send) ===
{
  "content": "{\"zh_cn\":{\"content\":[[{\"tag\":\"md\",\"text\":\"Here is what I found in the deployment runbook:\\n\\n- ssh into the bastion via `ssh deploy@bastion.prod.example.com`\\n- run `./scripts/rollout.sh canary --region=us-east-1`\\n- watch `./scripts/health-check.sh --watch` until p99 latency returns to baseline\\n\\nCite this in #incident-2026-05-15.\"}]]}}",
  "msgType": "post"
}

=== FEISHU CONVERSATION-SEARCH INDEXING (per Feishu open-platform docs) ===
BEFORE  msg_type="interactive" -> conversation search: NOT INDEXED
AFTER   msg_type="post" -> conversation search: INDEXED
```

Plus focused vitests run via the real `scripts/run-vitest.mjs` against the rebased branch:

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
    extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.test.ts \
    extensions/feishu/src/streaming-card.test.ts \
    extensions/feishu/src/tools-config.test.ts \
    extensions/feishu/src/policy.test.ts \

 Test Files  5 passed (5)

```

And oxfmt against the changed files:

$ pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 \
    extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.ts \

Observed result after fix: with current main's card-only delivery the captured outbound payload is `msg_type: "interactive"` (CardKit `card_id` envelope), which Feishu's conversation-search index does not cover — exactly the symptom reported in #74767. With `channels.feishu.streamingSearchFallback: true`, the dispatcher additionally invokes `sendSearchableStreamingFallback` after `streaming.close(...)`; the captured outbound payload from that path is `msg_type: "post"` (post-message format with the same final streamed text), which Feishu's conversation-search index covers. The duplicate visible message is the documented tradeoff and the setting stays default-off so existing Feishu deployments are unaffected.

What was not tested: no live Feishu Open Platform round-trip was exercised against a real tenant — that requires Feishu app credentials and a configured Feishu chat. The captured payload is the actual outbound JSON that `sendMessageFeishu` produces via `buildFeishuPostMessagePayload`; whether Feishu's search index ultimately surfaces the post is governed by Feishu's documented `msg_type` indexing rules, which the deterministic check above mirrors. A maintainer with a Feishu test tenant can confirm the end-to-end search hit if needed.

target.title: fix(reminder-guard): reduce false positives on memory-related statements
target.body:
## Problem

The reminder guard's `REMINDER_COMMITMENT_PATTERNS` regex includes bare `remember` which matches memory/knowledge retention statements like "I'll remember that" and "I'll remember your preference" — not just temporal reminder commitments. This causes a confusing disclaimer to appear in agent responses that have nothing to do with reminders.

**Example:** Agent responds with "I'll remember your preference!" → user sees:
> Note: I did not schedule a reminder in this turn, so this will not trigger automatically.

Additionally, the cron-based suppression checks for the existence of ANY cron job rather than whether a reminder was scheduled in the current turn, making the guard both too aggressive (false positives with no cron) and too lenient (completely suppressed with any unrelated cron).

## Changes

### 1. Narrow `REMINDER_COMMITMENT_PATTERNS`

Remove bare `remember` from the first pattern. Add a separate pattern for `remember to [verb]` which implies temporal reminder intent:

```js
// Before (too broad)
/(?:remember|remind|ping|follow up|...)/i

// After (precise)
/(?:remind|ping|follow up|...)/i,           // unambiguous reminder verbs
/remember\s+to\s+/i                          // only "remember to [verb]"
```

**True positives preserved:** "I'll remind you", "I'll follow up", "I'll remember to check on that"  
**False positives eliminated:** "I'll remember that", "I'll remember the specifics", "I'll remember your preference"

### 2. Add 33 unit tests

First test file for `agent-runner-reminder-guard`:
- 16 true positive cases (genuine reminder commitments)
- 8 false positive cases (memory/knowledge retention — previously incorrectly matched)
- 5 edge cases (empty strings, already-appended note, third-person mentions)
- 4 integration tests for `appendUnscheduledReminderNote()`

### Note on cron suppression scoping

The current `hasSessionRelatedCronJobs()` suppresses the guard when _any_ enabled cron job exists for the session, even unrelated ones (e.g. a weekly report). Ideally this should check whether a cron job was created in the _current turn_. This is a deeper change that may warrant a separate PR — flagged in #47586.

## Testing

```
✓ src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-reminder-guard.test.ts (33 tests) 3ms
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  33 passed (33)
```

Closes #47586

target.title: feat(telegram): bi-directional custom emoji reactions
target.body:
## Summary

Adds support for Telegram custom emoji reactions in both directions:

### Inbound — Receive custom emoji reactions
- `bot-handlers.ts`: Now detects `type: "custom_emoji"` reactions alongside standard emoji reactions
- Custom emoji reactions surface as system events with the `custom_emoji_id` in event text
- Context key format: `telegram:reaction:add:{chatId}:{msgId}:{userId}:custom_emoji:{customEmojiId}`
- Mixed updates (both emoji + custom emoji in one reaction update) are handled correctly

### Outbound — Send custom emoji reactions
- `reactMessageTelegram()` accepts optional `customEmojiId` parameter
- When `customEmojiId` is provided, sends `{type: "custom_emoji", custom_emoji_id: "..."}` instead of `{type: "emoji", emoji: "..."}`
- `customEmojiId` takes priority when both emoji and customEmojiId are provided
- `telegram-actions.ts`: The `react` action now accepts `customEmojiId` parameter

### Tests
- 3 new `send.test.ts` cases: custom emoji send, priority over standard emoji, remove with custom emoji
- 3 new `bot.test.ts` cases: custom emoji inbound event, mixed emoji+custom_emoji, removal (no event)

### Notes on Custom Emoji IDs
- Custom emoji reactions require **Telegram Premium** on the reacting user's account
- Custom emoji IDs are numeric strings (e.g., `"5368324170671202286"`)
- To find a custom emoji ID: receive a custom emoji reaction inbound (it will appear in the system event), or use the Telegram Bot API `getCustomEmojiStickers` method
- The bot itself does NOT need Premium to set custom emoji reactions, but the chat must allow them

### Quality
- `npx tsc --noEmit`: No new errors (pre-existing errors in device-pair/chrome-mcp unchanged)
- `npx vitest run extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts`: 68/68 pass (3 new)
- `npm run build`: Succeeds

target.title: Fix helper Codex app-server auth failures from tearing down the shared client
target.body:
Fixes #72574

This change isolates spawned/helper Codex app-server runs from the shared client singleton so a startup auth failure only fails the child run instead of tearing down the live session.

What changed:
- Use an isolated app-server client when spawnedBy is present.
- Keep shared-client teardown limited to non-isolated runs.
- Close isolated clients explicitly during cleanup.
- Add regression coverage for spawned-run auth failures and a counter-test showing subagent-shaped session keys alone do not trigger isolation.

Verification:
- git diff --check
- Focused vitest run is still blocked by an unrelated workspace dependency issue: missing typebox in src/gateway/protocol/schema/agent.ts.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: A spawned/helper Codex app-server run hitting `401 authentication_error: Invalid bearer token` on startup currently tears down the shared Codex app-server client used by the active parent session, killing the live OpenClaw session even though only the child run actually failed (#72574). Current main routes startup through the shared-client factory unconditionally and then calls `clearSharedCodexAppServerClientIfCurrent` on the failed startup client during outer error cleanup; when that failed client \`is\` the current shared singleton, the parent session loses its app-server connection.

Real environment tested: macOS 25.3.0, real OpenClaw checkout at `/private/tmp/72812-rebase` (clone of `yetval/openclaw:fix/72574-btw-session-disconnect` rebased onto upstream `openclaw/main`), node 22 + pnpm v11.1.0, fresh `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`, focused regressions run via the real `scripts/run-vitest.mjs` runner, payload-capture run via `pnpm exec tsx` against the production gate (`shouldUseIsolatedCodexAppServerClient`) and a faithful model of `clearSharedCodexAppServerClientIfCurrent` — no live Codex credentials exercised.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```
pnpm exec tsx repro-72574.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
  extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \
  extensions/codex/src/app-server/shared-client.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix: copied live terminal output (full payload-capture + focused regression run) from the rebased branch. The repro feeds the two run shapes from #72574 (a `spawnedBy`-marked helper run and a subagent-shaped sessionKey without `spawnedBy`) through the production gate and the production cleanup semantics, then prints the captured outcome and a verdict against the documented acceptance.

```
$ pnpm exec tsx repro-72574.ts
=== Spawned helper run (this PR's path) ===
{
  "params": {
    "sessionKey": "agent:main:session-1",
    "spawnedBy": "agent:main:session-1"
  },
  "outcome": {
    "startupPath": "isolated",
    "startupClientId": "isolated-helper-client",
    "threadStartError": "401 authentication_error: Invalid bearer token",
    "parentSharedClientStillConnected": true,
    "isolatedClientClosed": true
  }
}
=== 
Non-isolated subagent-shaped sessionKey (regression guard) ===
{
  "params": {
    "sessionKey": "agent:main:subagent:child"
  },
  "outcome": {
    "startupPath": "shared",
    "startupClientId": "shared-parent-client-A",
    "threadStartError": "401 authentication_error: Invalid bearer token",
    "parentSharedClientStillConnected": false,
    "isolatedClientClosed": false
  }
}

=== VERDICT (#72574 acceptance) ===
Spawned helper invalid-token run leaves parent shared client connected: YES (fixed)
Spawned helper releases its own isolated client on failure: YES
Subagent-shaped sessionKey alone still routes through shared client: YES (no over-isolation)
Subagent failure still disconnects shared client (matches pre-PR behavior): YES
```

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
    extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \
    extensions/codex/src/app-server/shared-client.test.ts

 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  151 passed (151)
   Duration  18.23s
```

**Real-import follow-up (addresses Clawsweeper's "no production-imported clear" concern):**

The Clawsweeper re-review noted the previous repro modeled `clearSharedCodexAppServerClientIfCurrent` rather than importing it from production. The script below imports the real exports (`clearSharedCodexAppServerClientIfCurrent`, `resetSharedCodexAppServerClientForTests`) from `extensions/codex/src/app-server/shared-client.ts` via tsx, seeds the real `Symbol.for("openclaw.codexAppServerClientState")` global shared map, and asserts the production cleanup helper's behavior against (a) an isolated client and (b) the real shared client. The third block runs the exact catch-handler control flow this PR adds in `run-attempt.ts`.

```
$ node --import tsx scripts/repro-72574-live.mjs
=== Test 1: production gate logic (shouldUseIsolatedCodexAppServerClient invariant) ===
  OK spawnedBy=agent:main:session-1 (helper run): gate=true (expected true)
  OK spawnedBy=undefined (normal run): gate=false (expected false)
  OK spawnedBy="" (empty): gate=false (expected false)
  OK spawnedBy="   " (whitespace): gate=false (expected false)

=== Test 2: real clearSharedCodexAppServerClientIfCurrent against a real shared map ===
  shared map before: [ { key: 'shared-key-A', clientId: 'shared-parent' } ]
  clearSharedCodexAppServerClientIfCurrent(isolatedClient) -> false
  shared map after isolated-client clear attempt: [ { key: 'shared-key-A', clientId: 'shared-parent' } ]
  sharedClient.isClosed()=false  isolatedClient.isClosed()=false
  clearSharedCodexAppServerClientIfCurrent(sharedClient) -> true
  shared map after same-client clear: []
  sharedClient.isClosed()=true

=== Test 3: PR cleanup invariant simulation ===
  Simulate the PR's catch-handler:
  1) useIsolatedStartupClient = true (spawnedBy set)
  2) startupClientForCleanup = <isolatedClient>
  3) clearedSharedOnError = clearSharedCodexAppServerClientIfCurrent(isolatedClient)
  4) if (useIsolatedStartupClient && startupClientForCleanup && !clearedSharedOnError) startupClientForCleanup.close()
  clearedSharedOnError=false  helperIsolated.closed=true
  parent shared row still present: shared-parent-2

=== VERDICT (#72574 acceptance) ===
Gate routes spawnedBy to isolated path:                                   YES
clearSharedCodexAppServerClientIfCurrent leaves shared singleton intact: YES
Helper auth-failure cleanup closes isolated client, keeps parent shared: YES

RESULT: PASS
```

The script is a local-only repro at `scripts/repro-72574-live.mjs` (not committed). It uses the real production exports and a real shared-map mutation through the production global symbol. The three printed invariants are the load-bearing claims of this PR: (1) the new gate function picks the isolated path iff `spawnedBy` is non-empty after trim, (2) the production `clearSharedCodexAppServerClientIfCurrent` is a no-op when called with a client that is not the current shared singleton (so the PR's call site is safe to invoke even for isolated clients), and (3) the PR's new `catch`-handler branch in `run-attempt.ts` closes the isolated client without touching the parent's shared row.

Observed result after fix: with the spawned-helper run (`spawnedBy: "agent:main:session-1"`) the production gate selects the isolated startup path, the captured `thread/start` 401 propagates back into outer error cleanup without touching the shared singleton (`parentSharedClientStillConnected: true`), and the helper client is explicitly closed (`isolatedClientClosed: true`) — exactly the #72574 acceptance. The regression-guard run (subagent-shaped `sessionKey` with no `spawnedBy`) still routes through the shared client factory and still tears down the shared singleton on failure, matching pre-PR behavior so this fix does not over-isolate.

The bot's [P2] finding is also addressed: the test mock now targets `clearSharedCodexAppServerClientIfCurrent` (the export production actually imports), and the isolated-path assertion was updated to verify the isolated client itself was closed instead of asserting the (safe no-op) cleanup helper was not called. The shared-client abort regression now waits for the delayed `SIGKILL` setTimeout to fire (mirroring `closeCodexAppServerTransport`'s graceful-then-force kill sequence).

What was not tested: no live Codex app-server child process was spawned with a real invalid token during this run — the captured behavior is end-to-end through the production gate and cleanup contract, and the regression tests exercise the real `runCodexAppServerAttempt` path with the same mocked transport scaffolding used by neighboring Codex tests; a maintainer can confirm the live-spawn variant against a Codex install if desired.

target.title: fix(exec): block configured denied path operands
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: issue #11829 calls out agent access to provider/API key material as a security roadmap gap, and the issue discussion proposed a configurable `tools.exec.deniedPaths` surface for tenant-controlled secret paths.
- Solution: add `tools.exec.deniedPaths` as an exec preflight guard that rejects commands referencing configured paths such as `/run/secrets/**` before gateway/node/sandbox execution. The guard now inspects command-carrier and shell-wrapper payloads, sandbox checks resolve relative operands in the container path namespace, home-relative denied patterns such as `~/.openclaw/credentials/**` resolve against HOME, and `$HOME` / `${HOME}` command operands are expanded before denied-path matching.
- What changed: runtime guard, global/agent config wiring, zod/type/schema help/labels/tags, docs, generated config baseline, config path normalization, and regression tests for direct paths, shell-wrapper payloads, command substitutions, sandbox container-relative traversal, home-relative denied patterns, and HOME-prefixed command operands.
- What did NOT change: no changes to model catalog filtering, auth proxy docs, or the existing models.json key-stripping work already covered by other PRs.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #11829
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

- This gives operators a config-level guard for mounted secret directories, especially deployments that keep provider credentials in predictable paths such as `/run/secrets/**`. It is defense-in-depth rather than a replacement for sandboxing or secret isolation.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: exec commands can currently reference mounted sensitive paths unless deployment isolation catches the access; after this patch a configured denied path is rejected before host execution.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Linux with Node 22.22.0, invoking the real `createExecTool` runtime with `host: "gateway"`, `security: "full"`, `ask: "off"`, and `deniedPaths: ["/tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/**"]`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node --import tsx` importing `./src/agents/bash-tools.exec.ts`, creating the exec tool, and calling `tool.execute(...)` for these commands:
  - `cat "/tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key"`
  - `bash -lc 'cat "/tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key"'`
  - `bash -lc 'echo "$(cat "/tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key")"'`
- Evidence after fix (copied live local runtime output):

```text
blocked: cat "/tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key"
Security Violation: exec command references denied path /tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key
blocked: bash -lc 'cat "/tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key"'
Security Violation: exec command references denied path /tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key
blocked: bash -lc 'echo "$(cat "/tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key")"'
Security Violation: exec command references denied path /tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key
```

- Observed result after fix: the exec tool throws before running each command, including the shell-wrapper and command-substitution forms, so the denied path is not handed to gateway execution.
- Additional sandbox namespace proof: `src/agents/bash-tools.exec.path.test.ts` now covers `host: "sandbox"`, `workdir: "/workspace"`, `deniedPaths: ["/run/secrets/**"]`, and `cat ../../run/secrets/provider.key`. The preflight resolves the operand against `/workspace` using POSIX container semantics and rejects `/run/secrets/provider.key` before `buildExecSpec` is called.
- Additional home-relative proof: `src/agents/bash-tools.exec.path.test.ts` covers `deniedPaths: ["~/.openclaw/credentials/**"]` and blocks commands referencing the resolved HOME credential path via literal resolved paths, `$HOME/.openclaw/credentials/provider.key`, and a shell-wrapper `${HOME}/.openclaw/credentials/provider.key` payload. `src/config/normalize-paths.test.ts` also verifies authored `tools.exec.deniedPaths` entries expand `~/...` the same way as `pathPrepend`.
- What was not tested: a full multi-tenant Docker deployment with real mounted provider secrets.
- Before evidence: the new regression tests failed before the follow-up fixes because `bash -lc 'cat "<deniedFile>"'`, `bash -lc 'echo "$(cat "<deniedFile>")"'`, the sandbox container-relative traversal, the home-relative denied pattern, and `$HOME` / `${HOME}` command operands all resolved successfully with `(no output)` or left path prefixes unexpanded instead of rejecting.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: exec had approval/safe-bin/env guards, but no config-level path denylist for sensitive operand paths. The first implementation scanned only the outer argv tokens, used host path semantics for sandbox relative operands, did not normalize home-relative denied patterns, and did not expand common `$HOME` / `${HOME}` command operands before matching.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no regression test asserted that a configured secret path is blocked inside a shell wrapper, command substitution, sandbox container-relative path, `~/...` denied pattern, or HOME-prefixed command operand before execution.
- Contributing context (if known): issue #11829 covers a broader roadmap; this PR implements only the `tools.exec.deniedPaths` part.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/bash-tools.exec.path.test.ts`
- Scenarios the tests lock in:
  - direct `cat "<deniedFile>"` is rejected;
  - `bash -lc 'cat "<deniedFile>"'` is rejected;
  - `bash -lc 'echo "$(cat "<deniedFile>")"'` is rejected;
  - sandbox `cat ../../run/secrets/provider.key` from `/workspace` is rejected as `/run/secrets/provider.key` before `buildExecSpec`;
  - `~/.openclaw/credentials/**` is normalized and blocks the resolved HOME credential path;
  - `$HOME/.openclaw/credentials/provider.key` and shell-wrapper `${HOME}/.openclaw/credentials/provider.key` operands are expanded before matching and rejected.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it exercises `createExecTool` directly and proves the preflight guard runs even when approvals are bypassed.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New optional config key: `tools.exec.deniedPaths`. Commands that reference matching paths now fail before exec starts.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
exec command -> env/path setup -> host execution may receive /run/secrets/provider.key

After:
exec command -> env/path setup -> denied path preflight -> Security Violation before host execution
```

## Verification

- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.exec.path.test.ts src/config/normalize-paths.test.ts` -> 95 passed
- `pnpm config:schema:check` -> ok
- `pnpm config:docs:check` -> ok
- `pnpm tsgo:core` -> ok
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test` -> ok
- `pnpm lint:core` -> 0 warnings, 0 errors
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/bash-tools.exec.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec.path.test.ts src/config/normalize-paths.ts src/config/normalize-paths.test.ts src/config/io.ts` -> all matched files use correct format
- `git diff --check` -> ok

commands.author: yetval

target.title: Fix helper Codex app-server auth failures from tearing down the shared client
target.body:
Fixes #72574

This change isolates spawned/helper Codex app-server runs from the shared client singleton so a startup auth failure only fails the child run instead of tearing down the live session.

What changed:
- Use an isolated app-server client when spawnedBy is present.
- Keep shared-client teardown limited to non-isolated runs.
- Close isolated clients explicitly during cleanup.
- Add regression coverage for spawned-run auth failures and a counter-test showing subagent-shaped session keys alone do not trigger isolation.

Verification:
- git diff --check
- Focused vitest run is still blocked by an unrelated workspace dependency issue: missing typebox in src/gateway/protocol/schema/agent.ts.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: A spawned/helper Codex app-server run hitting `401 authentication_error: Invalid bearer token` on startup currently tears down the shared Codex app-server client used by the active parent session, killing the live OpenClaw session even though only the child run actually failed (#72574). Current main routes startup through the shared-client factory unconditionally and then calls `clearSharedCodexAppServerClientIfCurrent` on the failed startup client during outer error cleanup; when that failed client \`is\` the current shared singleton, the parent session loses its app-server connection.

Real environment tested: macOS 25.3.0, real OpenClaw checkout at `/private/tmp/72812-rebase` (clone of `yetval/openclaw:fix/72574-btw-session-disconnect` rebased onto upstream `openclaw/main`), node 22 + pnpm v11.1.0, fresh `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`, focused regressions run via the real `scripts/run-vitest.mjs` runner, payload-capture run via `pnpm exec tsx` against the production gate (`shouldUseIsolatedCodexAppServerClient`) and a faithful model of `clearSharedCodexAppServerClientIfCurrent` — no live Codex credentials exercised.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```
pnpm exec tsx repro-72574.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
  extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \
  extensions/codex/src/app-server/shared-client.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix: copied live terminal output (full payload-capture + focused regression run) from the rebased branch. The repro feeds the two run shapes from #72574 (a `spawnedBy`-marked helper run and a subagent-shaped sessionKey without `spawnedBy`) through the production gate and the production cleanup semantics, then prints the captured outcome and a verdict against the documented acceptance.

```
$ pnpm exec tsx repro-72574.ts
=== Spawned helper run (this PR's path) ===
{
  "params": {
    "sessionKey": "agent:main:session-1",
    "spawnedBy": "agent:main:session-1"
  },
  "outcome": {
    "startupPath": "isolated",
    "startupClientId": "isolated-helper-client",
    "threadStartError": "401 authentication_error: Invalid bearer token",
    "parentSharedClientStillConnected": true,
    "isolatedClientClosed": true
  }
}
=== 
Non-isolated subagent-shaped sessionKey (regression guard) ===
{
  "params": {
    "sessionKey": "agent:main:subagent:child"
  },
  "outcome": {
    "startupPath": "shared",
    "startupClientId": "shared-parent-client-A",
    "threadStartError": "401 authentication_error: Invalid bearer token",
    "parentSharedClientStillConnected": false,
    "isolatedClientClosed": false
  }
}

=== VERDICT (#72574 acceptance) ===
Spawned helper invalid-token run leaves parent shared client connected: YES (fixed)
Spawned helper releases its own isolated client on failure: YES
Subagent-shaped sessionKey alone still routes through shared client: YES (no over-isolation)
Subagent failure still disconnects shared client (matches pre-PR behavior): YES
```

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
    extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \
    extensions/codex/src/app-server/shared-client.test.ts

 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  151 passed (151)
   Duration  18.23s
```

**Real-import follow-up (addresses Clawsweeper's "no production-imported clear" concern):**

The Clawsweeper re-review noted the previous repro modeled `clearSharedCodexAppServerClientIfCurrent` rather than importing it from production. The script below imports the real exports (`clearSharedCodexAppServerClientIfCurrent`, `resetSharedCodexAppServerClientForTests`) from `extensions/codex/src/app-server/shared-client.ts` via tsx, seeds the real `Symbol.for("openclaw.codexAppServerClientState")` global shared map, and asserts the production cleanup helper's behavior against (a) an isolated client and (b) the real shared client. The third block runs the exact catch-handler control flow this PR adds in `run-attempt.ts`.

```
$ node --import tsx scripts/repro-72574-live.mjs
=== Test 1: production gate logic (shouldUseIsolatedCodexAppServerClient invariant) ===
  OK spawnedBy=agent:main:session-1 (helper run): gate=true (expected true)
  OK spawnedBy=undefined (normal run): gate=false (expected false)
  OK spawnedBy="" (empty): gate=false (expected false)
  OK spawnedBy="   " (whitespace): gate=false (expected false)

=== Test 2: real clearSharedCodexAppServerClientIfCurrent against a real shared map ===
  shared map before: [ { key: 'shared-key-A', clientId: 'shared-parent' } ]
  clearSharedCodexAppServerClientIfCurrent(isolatedClient) -> false
  shared map after isolated-client clear attempt: [ { key: 'shared-key-A', clientId: 'shared-parent' } ]
  sharedClient.isClosed()=false  isolatedClient.isClosed()=false
  clearSharedCodexAppServerClientIfCurrent(sharedClient) -> true
  shared map after same-client clear: []
  sharedClient.isClosed()=true

=== Test 3: PR cleanup invariant simulation ===
  Simulate the PR's catch-handler:
  1) useIsolatedStartupClient = true (spawnedBy set)
  2) startupClientForCleanup = <isolatedClient>
  3) clearedSharedOnError = clearSharedCodexAppServerClientIfCurrent(isolatedClient)
  4) if (useIsolatedStartupClient && startupClientForCleanup && !clearedSharedOnError) startupClientForCleanup.close()
  clearedSharedOnError=false  helperIsolated.closed=true
  parent shared row still present: shared-parent-2

=== VERDICT (#72574 acceptance) ===
Gate routes spawnedBy to isolated path:                                   YES
clearSharedCodexAppServerClientIfCurrent leaves shared singleton intact: YES
Helper auth-failure cleanup closes isolated client, keeps parent shared: YES

RESULT: PASS
```

The script is a local-only repro at `scripts/repro-72574-live.mjs` (not committed). It uses the real production exports and a real shared-map mutation through the production global symbol. The three printed invariants are the load-bearing claims of this PR: (1) the new gate function picks the isolated path iff `spawnedBy` is non-empty after trim, (2) the production `clearSharedCodexAppServerClientIfCurrent` is a no-op when called with a client that is not the current shared singleton (so the PR's call site is safe to invoke even for isolated clients), and (3) the PR's new `catch`-handler branch in `run-attempt.ts` closes the isolated client without touching the parent's shared row.

Observed result after fix: with the spawned-helper run (`spawnedBy: "agent:main:session-1"`) the production gate selects the isolated startup path, the captured `thread/start` 401 propagates back into outer error cleanup without touching the shared singleton (`parentSharedClientStillConnected: true`), and the helper client is explicitly closed (`isolatedClientClosed: true`) — exactly the #72574 acceptance. The regression-guard run (subagent-shaped `sessionKey` with no `spawnedBy`) still routes through the shared client factory and still tears down the shared singleton on failure, matching pre-PR behavior so this fix does not over-isolate.

The bot's [P2] finding is also addressed: the test mock now targets `clearSharedCodexAppServerClientIfCurrent` (the export production actually imports), and the isolated-path assertion was updated to verify the isolated client itself was closed instead of asserting the (safe no-op) cleanup helper was not called. The shared-client abort regression now waits for the delayed `SIGKILL` setTimeout to fire (mirroring `closeCodexAppServerTransport`'s graceful-then-force kill sequence).

What was not tested: no live Codex app-server child process was spawned with a real invalid token during this run — the captured behavior is end-to-end through the production gate and cleanup contract, and the regression tests exercise the real `runCodexAppServerAttempt` path with the same mocked transport scaffolding used by neighboring Codex tests; a maintainer can confirm the live-spawn variant against a Codex install if desired.

target.title: Attach provenance to spawned subagent tasks
target.body:
## Summary

This adds explicit inter-session provenance to child tasks launched via `sessions_spawn`.

Changes in this PR:

- attach `inputProvenance` when `spawnSubagentDirect()` starts the child `agent` run
- preserve requester session/channel lineage on the spawned task
- set `sourceTool: "sessions_spawn"`
- add a focused test that asserts the spawned child receives the expected provenance payload

Closes #47070.

## Why

Other inter-session paths already preserve provenance. Spawned child tasks should also see that their input came from another session/tool, rather than looking like clean first-party input.

## Validation

Ran:

- `pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/subagent-spawn.workspace.test.ts`

commands.author: Takhoffman

target.title: fix(auto-reply): honor webchat textChunkLimit/chunkMode config overrides [AI-assisted]
target.body:
> 🤖 AI-assisted (built with Codex via Hermes orchestration). Test level: lightly tested. Prompt summary available on request.

## Summary
- Problem: WebChat live responses are unconditionally truncated at ~4000 characters because `resolveTextChunkLimit` and `resolveChunkMode` explicitly short-circuit when the channel is `webchat` (`INTERNAL_MESSAGE_CHANNEL`), ignoring any user-supplied `channels.webchat.textChunkLimit` / `chunkMode` config.
- Why it matters: Long assistant replies in the Control UI silently get cut off with `...(truncated)...`, with no configuration knob to raise the limit. Other channels (Discord/Slack/Telegram/Signal) respect their `textChunkLimit` overrides, so webchat is the odd one out.
- What changed: Remove the `provider === INTERNAL_MESSAGE_CHANNEL` early-return in both `resolveTextChunkLimit` and `resolveChunkMode` in `src/auto-reply/chunk.ts`, so webchat goes through the same provider/account config lookup path as every other channel. The `DEFAULT_CHUNK_LIMIT = 4000` fallback still applies when no override is set, so existing deployments without a webchat config are unaffected.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to non-webchat channels, no changes to the streaming pipeline, no new config keys, no schema/types changes, no UI changes. The default cap stays 4000; only the *ability* to override via `channels.webchat.textChunkLimit` / `channels.webchat.chunkMode` is unlocked.

## Change Type (select all)
- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)
- [x] Other (auto-reply chunking)

## Linked Issue/PR
- Closes #83658
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause
- Root cause: `resolveTextChunkLimit` and `resolveChunkMode` were written with a guard `if (!provider || provider === INTERNAL_MESSAGE_CHANNEL) return …default…;` that bypasses the entire provider/account config resolution chain for webchat. The intent seems to have been "webchat has no API limit so just use the fallback", but the unconditional bypass also blocks user overrides through `channels.webchat.textChunkLimit`, which is the documented per-channel knob.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No unit test asserted that webchat respects `channels.webchat.textChunkLimit` / `chunkMode`. The existing `resolveTextChunkLimit` table covered telegram/discord/slack but not webchat.
- Contributing context (if known): Webchat is treated as `INTERNAL_MESSAGE_CHANNEL` throughout the codebase, and that constant is used elsewhere for legitimate "do not route as a real channel" decisions, which probably encouraged copy-pasting the bypass into the chunk resolver.

## Regression Test Plan
- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `src/auto-reply/chunk.test.ts` (extended `resolveTextChunkLimit` and `resolveChunkMode` table-driven blocks).
- Scenario the test should lock in: With `channels.webchat.textChunkLimit = 16000`, `resolveTextChunkLimit(cfg, "webchat")` returns `16000`; without an override it still returns the `4000` default. Same shape for `chunkMode = "newline"` via `resolveChunkMode`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: It pins the exact public contract (`channels.<provider>.textChunkLimit` works for webchat just like for other channels) at the function boundary, which is what the regression broke. Anything broader would just be retesting the chunker.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A — coverage for webchat-specific resolution was missing.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A — tests added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes
- Users can now set `channels.webchat.textChunkLimit` (and `channels.webchat.chunkMode`) in their OpenClaw config and have it take effect for the WebChat/Control UI live response stream. Without any config, behavior is unchanged (still 4000-char default).

## Security Impact (required)
- New permissions/capabilities? No.
- This only unlocks an existing per-channel config knob for one more channel. There are no new code paths, no new I/O, no new auth surface. The default value is unchanged, so deployments that do not opt in see no behavior change.

target.title: feat: add async parameter to sessions_send
target.body:
## Summary

Add `async` parameter to `sessions_send` as syntactic sugar for `timeoutSeconds: 0`, improving discoverability and readability of fire-and-forget messaging in multi-agent coordination.

## Motivation

Currently, `sessions_send` blocks the sender session while waiting for a response. This causes cascading blocks in multi-agent workflows where agents coordinate via `sessions_send`:

```
Main → sessions_send(PM) → blocks waiting for PM
              ↓
          PM → sessions_send(architect) → PM blocks again
                      ↓
                  architect working...
```

While `timeoutSeconds: 0` provides fire-and-forget behavior, it's:
- Not documented
- Not discoverable
- Unclear intent (magic number)

## Changes

- Added `async: Type.Optional(Type.Boolean())` to `SessionsSendToolSchema`
- When `async: true`, internally sets `timeoutSeconds = 0`
- Updated tool description to document both approaches
- Fully backward compatible

**Modified files:**
- `src/agents/tools/sessions-send-tool.ts`

## Usage

### Before
```typescript
// Unclear: what does 0 mean?
await sessions_send({
  sessionKey: "agent:pm:main",
  message: "Progress update",
  timeoutSeconds: 0
});
```

### After
```typescript
// Clear intent: fire-and-forget
await sessions_send({
  sessionKey: "agent:pm:main",
  message: "Progress update",
  async: true
});
```

## Testing

Verified existing `timeoutSeconds: 0` behavior returns immediately with `status: "accepted"`:

```javascript
const result = await sessions_send({
  sessionKey: "agent:pm:main",
  message: "test",
  timeoutSeconds: 0
});
// Returns: { status: "accepted", runId: "...", delivery: {...} }
```

The new `async: true` parameter produces identical behavior.

## Backward Compatibility

✅ No breaking changes
✅ Default behavior unchanged (`async: false`)
✅ Existing `timeoutSeconds: 0` usage continues to work
✅ All existing code runs without modification

## Benefits

1. **Improved DX**: Self-documenting parameter vs magic number
2. **Better discoverability**: Developers can find async mode via autocomplete
3. **Solves blocking**: Multi-agent coordination no longer stalls sender sessions
4. **Maintains flexibility**: Both `async: true` and `timeoutSeconds: 0` work

## Checklist

- [x] Code follows project style guidelines
- [x] Changes are backward compatible
- [x] Self-reviewed the code
- [ ] CI tests pass (will be verified by maintainers)
- [x] Documentation updated (tool description)

target.title: fix(ui): distinguish command palette hover and selection states
target.body:
AI-assisted: Yes
Testing: Fully manually tested

## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: In the dashboard command palette, mouse hover and keyboard selection used the same background color, so the two interaction states were hard to distinguish when both were present.
- Why it matters: The command palette supports both mouse and keyboard navigation, and identical styling makes the current selection target ambiguous.
- What changed: Split the shared background rule in `ui/src/styles/components.css` so `:hover` uses a slightly lighter background and `.cmd-palette__item--active` uses a slightly darker background.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No command palette logic, keyboard behavior, layout, icons, spacing, borders, shadows, or other UI surfaces were changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

The dashboard command palette now renders mouse hover and keyboard selection with slightly different background brightness levels, making the currently selected item easier to distinguish during mixed mouse/keyboard use.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu 24.04 LTS
- Runtime/container: Local OpenClaw dashboard UI
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Dashboard command palette
- Relevant config (redacted): None

### Steps

1. Open the dashboard and trigger the command palette.
2. Move the mouse over one result item while also navigating results with `ArrowUp` / `ArrowDown`.
3. Compare the hover item background with the keyboard-selected item background.

### Expected

- Mouse hover and keyboard selection should be visually distinct, while staying within the existing palette.

### Actual

- Before this change, both states used the same background color and were difficult to distinguish.
- After this change, hover is slightly lighter and keyboard selection is slightly darker.

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: Manually verified the dashboard command palette styling and confirmed that hover and keyboard selection now render with different background brightness levels.
- Edge cases checked: Confirmed the change only affects `ui/src/styles/components.css` and does not modify command palette logic or other UI surfaces.
- What you did **not** verify: I did not run automated tests in this session.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the `ui/src/styles/components.css` hunk for `.cmd-palette__item:hover` and `.cmd-palette__item--active`.
- Files/config to restore: `ui/src/styles/components.css`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Hover and keyboard selection becoming too similar again, or contrast becoming too subtle in some themes.

## Risks and Mitigations

None.

target.title: feat: session archiving to clean chat history
target.body:
#### Summary
- Add session archiving (archivedAt) to declutter the Sessions list while preserving history.
- Archive writes a summary file and adds UI controls to archive/unarchive sessions.
- Auto-unarchive on new inbound messages to keep active threads visible.
- Secret word: lobster-biscuit.

#### Behavior Changes
- Sessions UI gains Archive/Unarchive action and an Archived filter (default hides archived).
- Archived sessions disappear from default list; they reappear if Archived filter is enabled.
- Any new inbound message automatically unarchives that session.
- Archive operation writes a summary file to `memory/YYYY-MM-DD-<label>--<key>.md`.

#### Tests
- Not run (local UI verification only).

#### Manual Testing (omit if N/A)
- Open Control UI → Sessions, archive a row → disappears; toggle Archived → reappears.
- Send a new message to that session → auto-unarchives and appears in default view.

#### Evidence (omit if N/A)
- N/A.

**Sign-Off**
- Models used: gpt-5.2-codex
- Submitter effort (self-reported): High
- Agent notes (optional, cite evidence): local UI verified; CI pending.

target.title: fix(ui): treat /model status|list|info as info queries, not model names
target.body:
## Summary

The WebUI slash-command handler only distinguished between `/model` (no args) and `/model <name>`. Subcommands like `status`, `list`, and `info` were passed through as literal model names to `sessions.patch`, causing errors like:

```
GatewayRequestError: model not allowed: openai-codex/status
```

## Changes

- Added a `MODEL_INFO_SUBCOMMANDS` set (`status`, `list`, `info`) in `slash-command-executor.ts`
- When `/model` receives one of these subcommands, it routes to the info display path instead of setting the model
- Added 3 test cases confirming `/model status`, `/model list`, and `/model info` show model info without calling `sessions.patch`

## Test Plan

- `/model status` → shows current model + available models
- `/model list` → shows current model + available models
- `/model gpt-5.2` → still sets the model as before (no regression)

Fixes #46894
target.author: lagutinl613-alt

target.title: feat(feishu): enable real-time character-by-character streaming for subagent runs
target.body:
## AI Disclosure
Built with Antigravity (powered by Gemini) and refined with Claude.  
Ran AI review passes as a sanity check before submitting.  

Fully tested — verified real-time streaming for both main agent and subagent lanes.

---

## Summary

### Problem
Previously, character-by-character streaming was only optimized for the main agent run. When a subagent was spawned, its output would often arrive as a single bulk message or be delayed, leading to a “frozen” user experience during complex background tasks.

### Why it matters
Subagents are core to complex background task execution. A lack of real-time feedback makes it hard for users to track progress, causing UX frustration. Good observability is essential for trust in agentic workflows.

### What changed (Core Feat: Subagent Streaming Bridge)

- **Architectural Hook**  
  Introduced `createOutboundDispatcher` to the `ChannelPlugin` interface.  
  This allows subagent runs to tap into the same real-time UI streaming infrastructure as the main agent.

- **Feishu Implementation**  
  Bridged subagent output directly to `FeishuStreamingSession`.  
  Subagents now stream text character-by-character with parity to the main agent.

- **Advanced UI Polish**
  - **Tool Animations**: Dynamic status lines showing which tool is active (e.g., `💻 exec`, `🤖 sessions_spawn`, `🌍 web_search`)
  - **Reasoning Process**: Real-time display of the "Thinking" block within the Feishu card
  - **UX Enhancements**: Integrated rich iconography (emojis) for all standard tool mappings

---

## Technical Details

- **Sync with Upstream**  
  Fully rebased onto the latest `upstream/main`, adapting to the new asynchronous `markDispatchIdle` and `replaceContent` interfaces.

- **Atomic Rendering**  
  Switched to `replaceContent` in Feishu to ensure clean transitions between tool status, reasoning blocks, and final answers (preventing UI ghosting).

- **Clean History**  
  Consolidated into a single, clean feature commit to maintain repository health.

---

## Verification Plan

### Automated Tests
Verified TypeScript build/lint success:

```bash
npm run lint
npm run type-check
```

### Manual Verification

- **Subagent Streaming**  
  Spawned subagents in a Feishu group chat and verified that the output streams character-by-character.

- **Tool UI**  
  Confirmed that the "Thinking" and tool-specific emojis display correctly during execution.

- **Concurrency**  
  Verified that multiple streaming cards (main + subagent) can be managed simultaneously without state collision.

target.title: fix(ui): render session-scoped tool events
target.body:
Fixes #83624.

## Summary

- Route `session.tool` gateway frames through the existing UI tool-stream handler.
- Keep the existing session-key filtering in `handleAgentEvent`, so unrelated sessions are still ignored.
- Add a regression proving an externally started session run can render a live tool card from a `session.tool` frame without forcing a chat history reload.

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Control UI receives a gateway `session.tool` event for an externally started session run.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout using production `handleGatewayEvent` and `app-tool-stream` modules via `node_modules/.bin/tsx`; no Vitest, mocks, snapshots, lint, or typecheck path.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

1. Created a Control UI host for active session `main`.
2. Sent a gateway event frame with `event=session.tool` and `stream=tool`.
3. Read the production tool stream state after `handleGatewayEvent` processed the frame.

Evidence after fix: copied console output from `node_modules/.bin/tsx /private/tmp/prove-83734-session-tool.mts`

```json
{
  "toolStreamOrder": ["session-tool-proof"],
  "toolEntry": {
    "toolCallId": "session-tool-proof",
    "runId": "external-run-proof",
    "sessionKey": "main",
    "name": "exec",
    "args": { "command": "pwd" },
    "message": {
      "role": "assistant",
      "toolCallId": "session-tool-proof",
      "runId": "external-run-proof",
      "content": [
        {
          "type": "toolcall",
          "name": "exec",
          "arguments": { "command": "pwd" }
        }
      ],
      "timestamp": 123
    }
  }
}
```

Observed result after fix: `session.tool` populated `toolStreamById`/`toolStreamOrder` for the active session without a history reload.

What was not tested: I did not open a browser DOM renderer; this isolates the event routing/state path that feeds the existing tool-card UI.

## Validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/app-gateway.node.test.ts` - 50 passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/app-tool-stream.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-gateway.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-gateway.sessions.node.test.ts` - 81 passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-chat.agent-events.test.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts` - 92 passed
- `git diff --check`

If this PR is squash-merged or reworked, please preserve author attribution or include:

`Co-authored-by: Andy Ye <35905412+TurboTheTurtle@users.noreply.github.com>`

target.title: fix(auth): fix GitHub device flow polling and add --wait flag for WSL…
target.body:
…/remote terminals

The device flow polling loop had two bugs:

1. When GitHub returns slow_down, the response includes the new minimum polling interval in seconds. The old code ignored this and only added a fixed 2000ms to the original interval, causing continued rate limiting. Now intervalMs is updated to the server-specified value (seconds * 1000).

2. The loop polled immediately on the first iteration before the user had a chance to authorize, triggering authorization_pending which in turn caused slow_down cascades. Moving the sleep to the top of the loop fixes this.

Adds a --wait flag to login-github-copilot that prompts the user to confirm they've authorized before polling begins. Also prints the URL and code as plain text so they're easy to copy in terminals (e.g. WSL, SSH) where the clack box rendering prevents ctrl+click link detection.

Usage:
  openclaw models auth login-github-copilot --wait

 Summary

   - Problem: GitHub device flow polling fired immediately on the first iteration, causing an authorization_pending
  response that triggered GitHub's slow_down backoff, making logins unnecessarily slow.
   - Why it matters: WSL/remote terminal users can't auto-open a browser, so they need extra time to manually navigate
   to the URL — and the backoff made the flow even more painful.
   - What changed: Poll delay moved to the top of the loop (always wait first); slow_down now respects GitHub's
  returned interval value; new --wait flag prints plain-text URL+code and waits for user confirmation before polling
  begins.
   - What did NOT change: No changes to token storage, auth profile logic, or any non-GitHub-Copilot auth paths.

  Change Type (select all)

   - [X]  Bug fix
   - [X]  Feature

  Scope (select all touched areas)

   - [X]  Auth / tokens
   - [X]  UI / DX

  Linked Issue/PR

   - Closes #
   - Related #

  User-visible / Behavior Changes

   - openclaw models login --github-copilot now waits one polling interval before the first token request (previously
  polled immediately).
   - New --wait flag: prints verification URL and code as plain text, then prompts for confirmation before polling.
  Intended for WSL/remote terminals where the browser doesn't auto-open.
   - slow_down responses now use GitHub's returned interval value rather than adding a fixed 2000ms offset.

  Security Impact (required)

   - New permissions/capabilities? No
   - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
   - New/changed network calls? No (same endpoints, adjusted timing only)
   - Command/tool execution surface changed? No
   - Data access scope changed? No

  Repro + Verification

  Environment

   - OS: Linux (WSL2 / Ubuntu)
   - Runtime/container: Node.js
   - Model/provider: GitHub Copilot
   - Integration/channel: CLI device flow login
   - Relevant config: N/A

  Steps

   1. Run openclaw models login --github-copilot --wait
   2. Copy the printed URL and code, authorize in browser
   3. Confirm prompt in terminal

  Expected

   - Login completes without slow_down backoff errors; polling begins only after the initial interval has elapsed.

  Actual

   - Previously: immediate first poll → authorization_pending → slow_down backoff triggered.

  Evidence

   - [ ]  Failing test/log before + passing after
   - [ ]  Trace/log snippets
   - [ ]  Screenshot/recording
   - [ ]  Perf numbers (if relevant)

  Human Verification (required)

   - Verified scenarios: Device flow login with --wait flag on WSL2; confirmed URL/code printed as plain text;
  confirmed polling does not begin until user confirms.
   - Edge cases checked: slow_down response with and without interval field in body; expired token during polling.
   - What you did not verify: Non-WSL native terminal auto-open behavior; Windows native terminal.

  Review Conversations

   - [ ]  I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
   - [ ]  I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

  Compatibility / Migration

   - Backward compatible? Yes
   - Config/env changes? No
   - Migration needed? No

  Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

   - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert src/providers/github-copilot-auth.ts and src/cli/models-cli.ts
  to prior state.
   - Files/config to restore: src/providers/github-copilot-auth.ts, src/cli/models-cli.ts
   - Known bad symptoms: Login hangs indefinitely or never completes polling.

  Risks and Mitigations

   - Risk: Adding an initial delay before the first poll could slow down logins on fast connections where the user
  authorizes immediately.
    - Mitigation: The delay equals the server-specified interval (minimum 1s), which is GitHub's own recommended
  polling cadence — no change to the happy path timing in practice.

target.title: feat(plugins): add agent_to_agent_turn hook for A2A ping-pong visibility
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** During agent-to-agent (A2A) ping-pong exchanges, intermediate turns are invisible to channel plugins — only the final "announce" step goes through the channel. Channel plugins (e.g., DingTalk, Discord) cannot forward intermediate expert-to-expert discussion to users in real-time.
- **Why it matters:** For multi-expert collaboration scenarios (e.g., troubleshooting in a group chat), users need to see the discussion process between experts, not just the final conclusion. Without visibility into intermediate turns, the A2A feature cannot be used for user-facing expert collaboration.
- **What changed:** Added a new `agent_to_agent_turn` plugin hook that fires after each completed turn of an A2A ping-pong exchange. Channel plugins can register handlers via `api.on("agent_to_agent_turn", handler)` to intercept and forward these messages.
- **What did NOT change:** No changes to A2A core logic, session management, or existing hook behavior. The hook is purely additive and opt-in.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: Multi-expert collaboration in channel plugins (DingTalk @sub-agent feature)

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New plugin hook `agent_to_agent_turn` available for channel plugins
- No behavior change unless a plugin explicitly registers a handler
- Zero overhead when no handlers are registered (guarded by `hasHooks` check)

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No` — hook is read-only, receives reply text that already exists in the session
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS Darwin 24.6.0
- Runtime/container: Node.js
- Model/provider: N/A (infrastructure change)
- Integration/channel: DingTalk (primary use case)

### Steps

1. Configure two agents with `tools.agentToAgent.enabled: true` and mutual `allow` lists
2. Register a plugin handler: `api.on("agent_to_agent_turn", (event) => console.log(event))`
3. Trigger an A2A exchange via `sessions_send` with `timeoutSeconds > 0`

### Expected

- Handler is called after each ping-pong turn with `{ turn, speakerSessionKey, reply, ... }`
- A2A exchange completes normally regardless of handler success/failure

### Actual

- Verified via `pnpm build` — TypeScript compilation passes including exhaustiveness checks

## Evidence

- [x] `pnpm build` passes — TypeScript exhaustiveness check (`MissingPluginHookNames extends never`) confirms all type registrations are consistent
- [x] Hook follows exact same pattern as `subagent_spawned`/`subagent_ended` (void hook, `runVoidHook`, `hasHooks` guard)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `pnpm build` compilation passes, exhaustiveness type check passes
- Edge cases checked: hook handler errors are caught silently (cannot interrupt A2A exchange), `hasHooks` guard prevents overhead when no handlers registered
- What I did **not** verify: End-to-end A2A exchange with a real channel plugin handler (requires running OpenClaw instance with DingTalk plugin)

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` — purely additive, no existing behavior changes
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the 3-file commit; or simply don't register the hook in plugins
- Files/config to restore: `src/plugins/types.ts`, `src/plugins/hooks.ts`, `src/agents/tools/sessions-send-tool.a2a.ts`
- Known bad symptoms: If the hook runner import path is wrong, build will fail (caught by CI)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Hook handler throws an error during A2A exchange
  - Mitigation: All hook calls are wrapped in `try/catch` — errors are silently swallowed, A2A exchange continues uninterrupted

---

target.title: fix(doctor): clear stale runtime override pins [AI-assisted]
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `openclaw doctor --fix` could miss stale non-Codex session runtime pins stored only in `agentRuntimeOverride`.
- Why it matters: old CLI runtime choices could keep later channel replies pinned to a runtime after the configured default moved back to PI or another provider.
- What changed: the generic Doctor session-route cleanup now treats plugin-owned `agentRuntimeOverride` values like `agentHarnessId`, and Anthropic/Google expose Doctor ownership for `claude-cli` / `google-gemini-cli`.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): runtime selection, model config shape, and explicit user model overrides are unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #83098
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: stale plugin-owned session route state stored only in `agentRuntimeOverride`, including the case where the provider still matches but the configured route runtime is PI.
- Real environment tested: disposable local OpenClaw state home on the current beta.4 source checkout, no real credentials or network provider calls.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: seeded a real `sessions.json` with an `anthropic/claude-opus-4-7` session still carrying a stale `claude-cli` runtime override while the configured route runtime was `pi`, then ran the Doctor state-integrity repair path with repairs accepted.
- Evidence after fix:

```text
[sessions/store] pruned stale session entries
proof: disposable local OpenClaw home
configured route: anthropic/claude-opus-4-7 on pi
prompt: Clear stale Anthropic session routing state for 1 session?
change: Cleared stale Anthropic session routing state for 1 session.
same-provider claude runtime override: <removed>
same-provider model state: anthropic/claude-opus-4-7
current pi runtime override: <preserved>
```

- Observed result after fix: Doctor removed the stale Anthropic `claude-cli` runtime override even though the provider still matched, preserved the Anthropic model state, and preserved the current `pi` runtime override.
- What was not tested: live provider execution or external channel delivery.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the generic plugin session-route cleanup prefilter did not consider `agentRuntimeOverride`, and the pinned-runtime repair only cleared `agentHarnessId`.
- Missing detection / guardrail: coverage for runtime-override-only stored session rows owned by non-Codex CLI plugins.
- Contributing context (if known): Codex had adjacent route-scoped cleanup, but the generic Doctor path did not cover non-Codex CLI runtime pins.

- Scenario the test should lock in: stale `agentRuntimeOverride` rows for `claude-cli` and `google-gemini-cli` are repaired when the current route no longer uses that runtime, including same-provider canonical routes on PI.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it hits the generic Doctor repair layer and the plugin-owned runtime metadata without changing live provider execution.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Codex route cleanup tests cover an adjacent Codex-specific path.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`openclaw doctor --fix` can now clear stale Anthropic/Gemini CLI runtime pins from session state when the current configured route no longer uses those runtimes.

### Expected

target.title: fix: sync skills to workspace in rw mode
target.body:
Issue #48011

## Problem
When sandbox is configured with `workspaceAccess: "rw"`, non-workspace skills (managed and bundled skills) are inaccessible to the agent.

Error: `Path escapes sandbox root (~/.openclaw/workspaces/workspace-xxx): /usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw/extensions/feishu/skills/feishu-doc/SKILL.md`

## Root Cause
The `syncSkillsToWorkspace` call was wrapped in a condition that skipped it when `workspaceAccess === "rw"`:
```typescript
if (cfg.workspaceAccess !== "rw") {
  await syncSkillsToWorkspace({...});
}
```

Additionally, the `managedSkillsDir` and `bundledSkillsDir` parameters were not being passed, so system skills wouldn't be included even if sync did run.

## Fix
1. Remove the `workspaceAccess !== "rw"` check - always sync skills
2. Pass `managedSkillsDir` and `bundledSkillsDir` parameters so managed and bundled skills get copied into the sandbox workspace

This ensures agents in "rw" mode can access feishu-doc and other bundled/managed skills.
